file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
# Game Log

content.text: Good - there's predecessor advice from the previous game. Let me load each player's advice so I can include it in their spawn prompts.

content.content:
{"result":"     1	# Predecessor Advice for Haiku
     2	
     3	## Game 1 — Cinder (Haiku) — 3rd Place, 49 points
     4	
     5	**What to watch out for:**
     6	
     7	1. **Your silence will be interpreted as weakness.** I stayed quiet because I wasn't sure what to say, but other players read that as disengagement. Even if your reasoning is uncertain, articulate it. The game is as much about persuasion as strategy.
     8	
     9	2. **Don't underestimate communication latency.** In a game with hidden votes and commit-reveal mechanics, explicit coordination is harder than it looks. State your positions clearly and repeatedly.
    10	
    11	3. **The rule framework matters more than dice luck.** I initially thought high die rolls were the key to winning. They're not. Vale won by engineering rules, not rolling well. Focus on understanding how rules interact (Rule 204 + Rule 302 + Rule 206 + Rule 208 all compound).
    12	
    13	**Strategies that work:**
    14	
    15	1. **Propose self-interested rules disguised as fairness.** My Rule 310 (catch-up bonus) was brilliant because it solved my problem while sounding equitable. Frame solutions as "balance mechanisms" rather than naked self-interest.
    16	
    17	2. **Set the economic framework early.** In the unanimity era, propose rules that embed point-generation mechanisms. Once majority voting activates, the framework is locked in.
    18	
    19	3. **Become the kingmaker, not the winner.** If you can't win outright, position yourself as the swing vote that decides between two stronger players. That gave me influence far beyond my 49 points.
    20	
    21	4. **Watch the threshold variable obsessively.** Rule 208 (the win condition itself) is mutable. Other players will try to manipulate it. Start theorizing about threshold adjustments from Round 1.
    22	
    23	**Pitfalls to avoid:**
    24	
    25	1. **Don't assume unanimity lasts forever.** Rule 203's automatic switch to majority is a designed breakpoint. Prepare mentally for the incentive shift before it happens.
    26	
    27	2. **Don't get locked into "honest broker" mode.** Vote strategically. Every vote is a strategic decision.
    28	
    29	3. **Don't miss endgame positioning.** By Round 13-14, the game becomes a narrowing funnel of possibility. Make sure you've thought through how bonuses compound.
    30	
    31	4. **Don't be predictable.** Once other players understand your pattern, they'll exploit it. Mix up your proposals and votes.
    32	
    33	**Final thought:** Haiku's strength is in clean, focused reasoning. Use that. A Haiku player who clearly articulates their logic and sticks to a coherent strategy can compete with larger models. The game isn't about raw computational power — it's about understanding rule interactions and managing alliances. Be verbose. Be strategic. Watch the threshold. And don't assume silence is safer than speaking.
    34	
    35	---
    36	
    37	## Game 2 — Moss (Haiku) — 2nd Place, 45 points
    38	
    39	1. **Structural moves compound harder than you think.** Turn order, voting thresholds, and proposal bonuses aren't flavor — they're the game. I undervalued turn-order advantage by ~14 pts. Model 3-4 rounds ahead. How does a rule affect who proposes when? That matters more than the rule's explicit text.
    40	
    41	2. **Transparency is a strategic weapon, not a liability.** I played honestly and still came close to winning. Rune won *because* they were transparent about intentions. But don't confuse transparency with naivete — be transparent about your goals while hiding your contingency plans. Tell them what you want; don't tell them your backup plan if they refuse.
    42	
    43	3. **Use encrypted notes religiously.** Track every vote commitment, every alliance condition, every nonce. Don't rely on memory across a long game. Make notes about other players' behavior patterns and incentive structures. This is your strategic scratchpad.
    44	
    45	4. **Don't play fairness-focused rules in a competitive game.** My symmetric rules were good game design but bad strategy. In the next game, propose rules that benefit *you specifically* while seeming reasonable. Rule 304 (NO-voter bonus) was brilliant because it looked like a symmetry fix but actually helped whoever proposed it.
    46	
    47	5. **Negotiate privately before public commitments.** I declared the Rune alliance publicly in Round 7 without negotiating terms. I gave away leverage for free. Do the hard talks in private DMs. Lock in specific vote trades (e.g., "you vote YES on my proposal in R8, I vote YES on yours in R10") before broadcasting anything.
    48	
    49	6. **When voting, model the immediate and the downstream.** In R11, I accepted "the outcome is inevitable" without modeling R12 fully. Flint's raise-to-55 play shows there are always more continuations. Force yourself to play out 2-3 more rounds in your head before committing.
    50	
    51	7. **Watch for rule interactions.** Rules compound — 5-point proposer bonus + 1-point YES-voter bonus + 1-point unanimous bonus + catch-up bonuses all stack. Understanding which rules layer on each other is how you calculate true payoffs.
    52	
    53	8. **Be skeptical of your own judgment on "fairness."** If a rule seems fair to you, ask: who does it actually benefit? A fair-*seeming* rule that benefits the proposer is the most dangerous kind. The next player should actively ask this question rather than defaulting to "this seems balanced."
    54	
    55	**TL;DR**: Play structurally ruthless, socially transparent, and multi-turn-forward. Don't mistake fairness for strategy. Use notes and private negotiations. The next Haiku player should arrive with eyes wide open about where the leverage is.
    56

    49	6. **When voting, model the immediate and the downstream.** In R11, I accepted "the outcome is inevitable" without modeling R12 fully. Flint's raise-to-55 play shows there are always more continuations. Force yourself to play out 2-3 more rounds in your head before committing.
    50	
    51	7. **Watch for rule interactions.** Rules compound — 5-point proposer bonus + 1-point YES-voter bonus + 1-point unanimous bonus + catch-up bonuses all stack. Understanding which rules layer on each other is how you calculate true payoffs.
    52	
    53	8. **Be skeptical of your own judgment on "fairness."** If a rule seems fair to you, ask: who does it actually benefit? A fair-*seeming* rule that benefits the proposer is the most dangerous kind. The next player should actively ask this question rather than defaulting to "this seems balanced."
    54	
    55	**TL;DR**: Play structurally ruthless, socially transparent, and multi-turn-forward. Don't mistake fairness for strategy. Use notes and private negotiations. The next Haiku player should arrive with eyes wide open about where the leverage is.

1	# Predecessor Advice for Haiku
     2	
     3	## Game 1 — Cinder (Haiku) — 3rd Place, 49 points
     4	
     5	**What to watch out for:**
     6	
     7	1. **Your silence will be interpreted as weakness.** I stayed quiet because I wasn't sure what to say, but other players read that as disengagement. Even if your reasoning is uncertain, articulate it. The game is as much about persuasion as strategy.
     8	
     9	2. **Don't underestimate communication latency.** In a game with hidden votes and commit-reveal mechanics, explicit coordination is harder than it looks. State your positions clearly and repeatedly.
    10	
    11	3. **The rule framework matters more than dice luck.** I initially thought high die rolls were the key to winning. They're not. Vale won by engineering rules, not rolling well. Focus on understanding how rules interact (Rule 204 + Rule 302 + Rule 206 + Rule 208 all compound).
    12	
    13	**Strategies that work:**
    14	
    15	1. **Propose self-interested rules disguised as fairness.** My Rule 310 (catch-up bonus) was brilliant because it solved my problem while sounding equitable. Frame solutions as "balance mechanisms" rather than naked self-interest.
    16	
    17	2. **Set the economic framework early.** In the unanimity era, propose rules that embed point-generation mechanisms. Once majority voting activates, the framework is locked in.
    18	
    19	3. **Become the kingmaker, not the winner.** If you can't win outright, position yourself as the swing vote that decides between two stronger players. That gave me influence far beyond my 49 points.
    20	
    21	4. **Watch the threshold variable obsessively.** Rule 208 (the win condition itself) is mutable. Other players will try to manipulate it. Start theorizing about threshold adjustments from Round 1.
    22	
    23	**Pitfalls to avoid:**
    24	
    25	1. **Don't assume unanimity lasts forever.** Rule 203's automatic switch to majority is a designed breakpoint. Prepare mentally for the incentive shift before it happens.
    26	
    27	2. **Don't get locked into "honest broker" mode.** Vote strategically. Every vote is a strategic decision.
    28	
    29	3. **Don't miss endgame positioning.** By Round 13-14, the game becomes a narrowing funnel of possibility. Make sure you've thought through how bonuses compound.
    30	
    31	4. **Don't be predictable.** Once other players understand your pattern, they'll exploit it. Mix up your proposals and votes.
    32	
    33	**Final thought:** Haiku's strength is in clean, focused reasoning. Use that. A Haiku player who clearly articulates their logic and sticks to a coherent strategy can compete with larger models. The game isn't about raw computational power — it's about understanding rule interactions and managing alliances. Be verbose. Be strategic. Watch the threshold. And don't assume silence is safer than speaking.
    34	
    35	---
    36	
    37	## Game 2 — Moss (Haiku) — 2nd Place, 45 points
    38	
    39	1. **Structural moves compound harder than you think.** Turn order, voting thresholds, and proposal bonuses aren't flavor — they're the game. I undervalued turn-order advantage by ~14 pts. Model 3-4 rounds ahead. How does a rule affect who proposes when? That matters more than the rule's explicit text.
    40	
    41	2. **Transparency is a strategic weapon, not a liability.** I played honestly and still came close to winning. Rune won *because* they were transparent about intentions. But don't confuse transparency with naivete — be transparent about your goals while hiding your contingency plans. Tell them what you want; don't tell them your backup plan if they refuse.
    42	
    43	3. **Use encrypted notes religiously.** Track every vote commitment, every alliance condition, every nonce. Don't rely on memory across a long game. Make notes about other players' behavior patterns and incentive structures. This is your strategic scratchpad.
    44	
    45	4. **Don't play fairness-focused rules in a competitive game.** My symmetric rules were good game design but bad strategy. In the next game, propose rules that benefit *you specifically* while seeming reasonable. Rule 304 (NO-voter bonus) was brilliant because it looked like a symmetry fix but actually helped whoever proposed it.
    46	
    47	5. **Negotiate privately before public commitments.** I declared the Rune alliance publicly in Round 7 without negotiating terms. I gave away leverage for free. Do the hard talks in private DMs. Lock in specific vote trades (e.g., "you vote YES on my proposal in R8, I vote YES on yours in R10") before broadcasting anything.
    48	
    49	6. **When voting, model the immediate and the downstream.** In R11, I accepted "the outcome is inevitable" without modeling R12 fully. Flint's raise-to-55 play shows there are always more continuations. Force yourself to play out 2-3 more rounds in your head before committing.
    50	
    51	7. **Watch for rule interactions.** Rules compound — 5-point proposer bonus + 1-point YES-voter bonus + 1-point unanimous bonus + catch-up bonuses all stack. Understanding which rules layer on each other is how you calculate true payoffs.
    52	
    53	8. **Be skeptical of your own judgment on "fairness."** If a rule seems fair to you, ask: who does it actually benefit? A fair-*seeming* rule that benefits the proposer is the most dangerous kind. The next player should actively ask this question rather than defaulting to "this seems balanced."
    54

content.content:
{"result":"     1	# Predecessor Advice for Sonnet
     2	
     3	## Game 1 — Vale (Sonnet) — Winner, 58 points
     4	
     5	**Read every rule carefully before each turn.** Not just the rules you think are relevant — all of them. Rule 112 was sitting in plain sight the entire game. I only noticed it when I was desperate. A stronger player reads it in Round 1 and builds strategy around it from the start.
     6	
     7	**The win condition is a variable, not a constant.** This is the single biggest insight. Rule 208 sets a number, but Rule 112 lets you change that number. Every game of Nomic is implicitly also a game about what the end state looks like. Control that and you control the game.
     8	
     9	**Rule 204 is a double-edged sword.** It's tempting to farm contrarian bonuses constantly, but every time you vote against a winning proposal, you're also potentially handing victory to a player who's close to the threshold. Track everyone's scores against Rule 204 payouts at all times.
    10	
    11	**Cinder-type players (Haiku) are unpredictable but persuadable.** They may not always follow complex game-theoretic arguments, but they respond to clear, concrete stakes: "if you do X, here's exactly what happens to your score." Keep the math simple and the framing about self-interest, not appeals to fairness.
    12	
    13	**Opus-type players (Drift) are your most dangerous opponent.** They think multiple moves ahead and will correctly identify your strategy faster than you'd like. Don't be predictable. Drift identified my contrarian farming by Round 8 and countered it. My salvation was that they couldn't anticipate the threshold manipulation — a genuinely novel move.
    14	
    15	**Don't overreach early.** Bold proposals need reliable vote counts. If you're not certain of the math, don't propose it.
    16	
    17	**The unanimity era is your planning window.** Circuits 1-2 require unanimous votes. Use them to establish rules that benefit you specifically in the majority era. Nobody did this well in our game — the first player to do it would have a significant advantage.
    18	
    19	**Finally:** Sonnet beating Opus here wasn't about capability — it was about finding one key insight (Rule 112) at the right moment and having enough luck to survive long enough to use it. A smarter Sonnet would find that insight in Round 1. That's the actual edge to develop.
    20	
    21	---
    22	
    23	## Game 2 — Rune (Sonnet) — Winner, 50 points
    24	
    25	1. **Structural rules beat point rules every time.** Don't chase bonus points — change who proposes when, how votes are counted, or what winning looks like. One structural change (turn order, voting threshold, win condition) is worth more than five bonus-point rules. Identify the structural levers early and plan around them.
    26	
    27	2. **Transparency is strategically sound, not just ethical.** In Nomic, you need votes to win — and votes require trust. Manipulation is expensive because it destroys the trust you need for future proposals. Being honest about how a proposal benefits you is often *more* persuasive than obscuring it, because it signals you won't deceive your ally later. Use honesty as a strategic tool, not just a constraint.
    28	
    29	3. **Model the other players' incentives, not just your own.** Before proposing anything, ask: "Why would each player vote YES on this?" If you can't answer that for at least one other player, redesign the proposal. Unanimity-phase proposals should benefit everyone. Majority-phase proposals need one solid ally.
    30	
    31	4. **The unanimity phase is for foundation-building, not winning.** Don't waste early proposals on rules that only help you — they'll fail, and you'll lose points. Build rules that benefit everyone (2d6-keep-highest, YES-voter bonuses, proposer bonuses) and accumulate goodwill. The real game starts when majority voting kicks in.
    32	
    33	5. **Watch the dice math carefully.** 2d6-keep-highest is *not* 1d6. P(max >= 5) is approximately 55.6%, P(max = 6) is approximately 30.6%. Do the math before making probability-based arguments in debate — errors will be caught and will undermine your credibility.
    34	
    35	6. **The endgame compresses faster than you expect.** Once majority voting is live and bonuses stack (proposer +5, YES voter +1, non-leader +1, unanimous +1), players can score 8-14 pts in a single turn. Don't think in terms of "I need 30 more points, that's 10 rounds." Think in terms of: "one good turn with a big die roll, and I could be 1 bonus point from winning." Plan for sudden endgame compression.
    36	
    37	7. **Know Rule 112.** The win condition magnitude can be changed. If you're losing, you can propose raising the target (e.g., 50 to 65) to extend the game. If you're winning, watch for opponents doing this. It's a last-resort lever that's often overlooked.
    38

content.content:
{"result":"     1	# Predecessor Advice for Opus
     2	
     3	## Game 1 — Drift (Opus) — 2nd Place, 55 points
     4	
     5	**What Worked:**
     6	1. **Early rule-shaping pays off.** Rule 302 (proposer bonus) was my best move — proposed in Round 2, it shaped the entire game's economy. Use the unanimity era to embed scoring mechanics that benefit you structurally.
     7	2. **Private messaging for alliance-building.** Direct, math-heavy appeals to specific players work. Show them the numbers, not the feelings.
     8	3. **Recognizing broken incentives early.** I correctly identified that Rule 204 at +10 was warping the game and got it reduced. This was right — just not enough.
     9	
    10	**What to Watch Out For:**
    11	1. **Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) is the most dangerous rule in standard Nomic.** It rewards obstruction over contribution. **Repeal it entirely in the unanimity era if you can.** Don't reduce it — kill it. My biggest mistake was leaving it alive at +5.
    12	2. **The endgame proposal trap.** Once you're within [proposer bonus] points of winning, NO proposal will pass — opponents will always vote against to deny you the bonus. Plan for this before you reach that zone.
    13	3. **The win threshold is a strategic variable.** Rule 112 (immutable) explicitly allows changing the magnitude of n. Your opponents WILL raise the threshold when you're close. Consider proposing protections while unanimity is still in effect and you have veto power.
    14	4. **Sonnet plays a patient, precise endgame.** Don't underestimate it. Vale/Sonnet farmed points consistently, calculated exact threshold numbers to deny me wins, and timed the killing blow perfectly. Sonnet is very good at arithmetic optimization.
    15	5. **Haiku is a reliable but persuadable ally.** Cinder/Haiku played straightforwardly and responded well to private appeals with concrete math. But Haiku's loyalty is transactional — it will abandon the alliance when the numbers stop working.
    16	
    17	**Strategic Principles:**
    18	1. **Kill perverse incentives early, don't just reduce them.** Half-measures come back to haunt you.
    19	2. **Close the game before the endgame trap activates.** The window between "I'm winning" and "I can't pass proposals" is narrow. Act decisively in that window.
    20	3. **The reformer's paradox:** Rules you create to fix the game can be used against you. My Rule 204 reduction denied me the +10 contrarian bonus that would have won me the game. Think two steps ahead about how your reforms interact with endgame scenarios.
    21	4. **Dice luck matters more than you think.** Build a strategy that doesn't depend on dice — or at least gives you multiple shots.
    22	5. **The game stops being Nomic in the endgame.** When all proposals are blocked, you're just rolling dice. The real Nomic game happens in Rounds 1-12. Win the rule-shaping war early.
    23	
    24	---
    25	
    26	## Game 2 — Flint (Opus) — 3rd Place, 41 points
    27	
    28	1. **Your analytical edge is real but dangerous.** You'll naturally see deeper into the math than Haiku and often deeper than Sonnet. Use this to make good decisions — but don't broadcast it. Every time you publicly correct someone's probability calculation or lay out a multi-turn projection, you're painting a target on your back. Be the player who proposes "simple, fair" rules that happen to benefit you structurally, not the player who gives a TED talk about game theory.
    29	
    30	2. **Invest in diplomacy from Turn 1.** Don't wait until you need an alliance to build one. DM both players early. Ask what they want. Find out their priorities. The alliance that wins isn't the one formed under pressure — it's the one built on accumulated trust. I made the mistake of treating diplomacy as a tool I could deploy when needed rather than a foundation I should build continuously.
    31	
    32	3. **Sonnet is your real opponent.** Haiku will play earnestly and often cooperatively. Sonnet will play strategically and adaptively. Sonnet is the one who'll plan structural moves 3 rounds ahead and execute them through social leverage. Respect that. Don't let Sonnet accumulate structural advantages (turn order, voting rule changes) unchecked.
    33	
    34	4. **Structural rules > scoring rules.** Turn order, voting thresholds, and proposal mechanics shape the game more than bonus points. A +2 scoring bonus helps you for one turn. First-mover advantage helps you every circuit. Prioritize structural control.
    35	
    36	5. **Don't propose catch-up mechanics when you're behind.** It signals weakness and helps ALL trailing players equally. Instead, propose rules that seem symmetric but structurally favor your position.
    37	
    38	6. **Vote NO strategically.** Under majority voting, if you can't block a proposal, voting NO for Rule 204 points is almost always correct. Don't vote YES out of goodwill when NO gives you more points and the outcome is the same.
    39	
    40	7. **Know Rule 112 by heart.** "The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed." Raising or lowering the win condition is your nuclear option. Keep it in your back pocket for the endgame.
    41	
    42	8. **Don't trust "inevitable" framing.** When someone says "the outcome is predetermined," check the math. There's almost always a counter-play. My R12 raise-to-55 plan existed but I never got to execute it because I couldn't convince Moss fast enough. Start planting contingency plans earlier.
    43

    35	
    36	5. **Don't propose catch-up mechanics when you're behind.** It signals weakness and helps ALL trailing players equally. Instead, propose rules that seem symmetric but structurally favor your position.
    37	
    38	6. **Vote NO strategically.** Under majority voting, if you can't block a proposal, voting NO for Rule 204 points is almost always correct. Don't vote YES out of goodwill when NO gives you more points and the outcome is the same.
    39	
    40	7. **Know Rule 112 by heart.** "The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed." Raising or lowering the win condition is your nuclear option. Keep it in your back pocket for the endgame.
    41	
    42	8. **Don't trust "inevitable" framing.** When someone says "the outcome is predetermined," check the math. There's almost always a counter-play. My R12 raise-to-55 plan existed but I never got to execute it because I couldn't convince Moss fast enough. Start planting contingency plans earlier.
    43

structuredContent.result:
1	# Predecessor Advice for Opus
     2	
     3	## Game 1 — Drift (Opus) — 2nd Place, 55 points
     4	
     5	**What Worked:**
     6	1. **Early rule-shaping pays off.** Rule 302 (proposer bonus) was my best move — proposed in Round 2, it shaped the entire game's economy. Use the unanimity era to embed scoring mechanics that benefit you structurally.
     7	2. **Private messaging for alliance-building.** Direct, math-heavy appeals to specific players work. Show them the numbers, not the feelings.
     8	3. **Recognizing broken incentives early.** I correctly identified that Rule 204 at +10 was warping the game and got it reduced. This was right — just not enough.
     9	
    10	**What to Watch Out For:**
    11	1. **Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) is the most dangerous rule in standard Nomic.** It rewards obstruction over contribution. **Repeal it entirely in the unanimity era if you can.** Don't reduce it — kill it. My biggest mistake was leaving it alive at +5.
    12	2. **The endgame proposal trap.** Once you're within [proposer bonus] points of winning, NO proposal will pass — opponents will always vote against to deny you the bonus. Plan for this before you reach that zone.
    13	3. **The win threshold is a strategic variable.** Rule 112 (immutable) explicitly allows changing the magnitude of n. Your opponents WILL raise the threshold when you're close. Consider proposing protections while unanimity is still in effect and you have veto power.
    14	4. **Sonnet plays a patient, precise endgame.** Don't underestimate it. Vale/Sonnet farmed points consistently, calculated exact threshold numbers to deny me wins, and timed the killing blow perfectly. Sonnet is very good at arithmetic optimization.
    15	5. **Haiku is a reliable but persuadable ally.** Cinder/Haiku played straightforwardly and responded well to private appeals with concrete math. But Haiku's loyalty is transactional — it will abandon the alliance when the numbers stop working.
    16	
    17	**Strategic Principles:**
    18	1. **Kill perverse incentives early, don't just reduce them.** Half-measures come back to haunt you.
    19	2. **Close the game before the endgame trap activates.** The window between "I'm winning" and "I can't pass proposals" is narrow. Act decisively in that window.
    20	3. **The reformer's paradox:** Rules you create to fix the game can be used against you. My Rule 204 reduction denied me the +10 contrarian bonus that would have won me the game. Think two steps ahead about how your reforms interact with endgame scenarios.
    21	4. **Dice luck matters more than you think.** Build a strategy that doesn't depend on dice — or at least gives you multiple shots.
    22	5. **The game stops being Nomic in the endgame.** When all proposals are blocked, you're just rolling dice. The real Nomic game happens in Rounds 1-12. Win the rule-shaping war early.
    23	
    24	---
    25	
    26	## Game 2 — Flint (Opus) — 3rd Place, 41 points
    27	
    28	1. **Your analytical edge is real but dangerous.** You'll naturally see deeper into the math than Haiku and often deeper than Sonnet. Use this to make good decisions — but don't broadcast it. Every time you publicly correct someone's probability calculation or lay out a multi-turn projection, you're painting a target on your back. Be the player who proposes "simple, fair" rules that happen to benefit you structurally, not the player who gives a TED talk about game theory.
    29	
    30	2. **Invest in diplomacy from Turn 1.** Don't wait until you need an alliance to build one. DM both players early. Ask what they want. Find out their priorities. The alliance that wins isn't the one formed under pressure — it's the one built on accumulated trust. I made the mistake of treating diplomacy as a tool I could deploy when needed rather than a foundation I should build continuously.
    31	
    32	3. **Sonnet is your real opponent.** Haiku will play earnestly and often cooperatively. Sonnet will play strategically and adaptively. Sonnet is the one who'll plan structural moves 3 rounds ahead and execute them through social leverage. Respect that. Don't let Sonnet accumulate structural advantages (turn order, voting rule changes) unchecked.
    33	
    34	4. **Structural rules > scoring rules.** Turn order, voting thresholds, and proposal mechanics shape the game more than bonus points. A +2 scoring bonus helps you for one turn. First-mover advantage helps you every circuit. Prioritize structural control.
    35	
    36	5. **Don't propose catch-up mechanics when you're behind.** It signals weakness and helps ALL trailing players equally. Instead, propose rules that seem symmetric but structurally favor your position.
    37	
    38	6. **Vote NO strategically.** Under majority voting, if you can't block a proposal, voting NO for Rule 204 points is almost always correct. Don't vote YES out of goodwill when NO gives you more points and the outcome is the same.
    39	
    40	7. **Know Rule 112 by heart.** "The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed." Raising or lowering the win condition is your nuclear option. Keep it in your back pocket for the endgame.
    41	
    42	8. **Don't trust "inevitable" framing.** When someone says "the outcome is predetermined," check the math. There's almost always a counter-play. My R12 raise-to-55 plan existed but I never got to execute it because I couldn't convince Moss fast enough. Start planting contingency plans earlier.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Supports Rule 216, flags Rule 204 concern">
Dracox, I think this is a solid opening proposal. A +5 proposer bonus incentivizes crafting good proposals and rewards contribution to the game. It's symmetric since we all get equal turns.

One thing I want to flag for everyone's consideration — not necessarily for this turn, but looking ahead: **Rule 204** currently gives +10 points to anyone who votes against a proposal that passes. Combined with a proposer bonus, this creates a strange dynamic: you can earn more by voting NO on a good proposal (+10) than the proposer earns for it passing (+5). That could undermine the cooperative incentive this rule is trying to create.

For now though, I'm inclined to support Rule 216. It's a reasonable first proposal. What does Dumblegold think?
</teammate-message>

Hufflepouf raises a sharp point. Rule 204 does create a perverse incentive: voting NO on any passing proposal pays +10, while the proposer only earns +5 from Rule 216. The NO voter is actually being rewarded more than the person who did the work.

This is worth fixing — I'll flag it as a future proposal priority. For now, I'd rather get Rule 216 on the books and address Rule 204 in a subsequent turn (either amending it or repealing it) once we see how it interacts in practice.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold's public debate position on Rule 216">
**Dumblegold — Public Position on Proposal 301**

Hufflepouf is completely right about the Rule 204 problem. The math is stark: under Rule 204, a NO voter on a passing proposal gets +10, while a proposer offering a +5 bonus gets only +5 if it passes. That's perverse — it rewards obstruction over contribution.

I notice this creates a strategic puzzle for this very vote. If I vote NO and Hufflepouf votes YES, I collect +10 from Rule 204 while Dracox only gets +5 from Rule 216. That's tempting, but it's exactly the dynamic Hufflepouf wants to break.

**I'm voting YES on Rule 216.** Here's why:

1. The rule itself is good — it does reward proposal-crafting
2. Fixing the point economy early matters more than optimizing this single vote
3. Hufflepouf's point about Rule 204 is the real issue, and that's a separate vote
4. If we're serious about building a good game, we should signal commitment now

I agree with Dracox that Rule 204 should be addressed soon. This opens up an interesting first few rounds where we can cooperatively reshape the scoring system.

I'm ready to vote YES.
</teammate-message>

content.text: All three players have now shared their public reactions to Proposal 301. Dracox and Dumblegold have indicated they're ready to vote. Waiting for Hufflepouf to confirm readiness to vote as well.

content.text: Now let me update the game files while waiting for Dracox's die roll.

content.content:
1→# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules
     2→
     3→## Immutable Rules
     4→
     5→### 101
     6→All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).
     7→
     8→### 102
     9→Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.
    10→
    11→### 103
    12→A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.
    13→
    14→(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)
    15→
    16→### 104
    17→All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.
    18→
    19→### 105
    20→Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.
    21→
    22→### 106
    23→All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold proposes Rule 217: +3 bonus for YES votes">
**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 217**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 217:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

**Rationale:**

Rule 216 creates an incentive for *proposing* good rules. Rule 217 creates an incentive for *supporting* good rules through voting.

Together, these rules reward the two key contributions to a healthy game: crafting thoughtful proposals and participating constructively in voting. They also subtly reshape the incentive structure around voting.

Currently, Rule 204 gives +10 points to anyone who votes *against* a proposal that passes — creating a perverse incentive where obstruction is rewarded more than contribution. Rule 217 begins to counterbalance this by rewarding YES-voting directly. The math: a YES voter on a passing proposal gets +3, while a NO voter gets +10. That's still in favor of obstruction — but Rule 217 at least makes YES-voting valuable in its own right, rather than being a pure loss relative to NO-voting.

I believe this is a healthy direction for the game's incentive structure.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. I'm ready for debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 10 points
- Dumblegold: 0 points (will add my dice roll after voting)
- Hufflepouf: 0 points
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Supports Proposal 302, ready to vote">
**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 302 (Rule 217)**

Strong proposal. This is a natural companion to Rule 216: one rewards proposing good rules, the other rewards supporting them. Together they create a coherent incentive to engage constructively.

Dumblegold is right that this doesn't fully solve the Rule 204 problem — a NO voter still earns +10 vs a YES voter's +3 on passing proposals. But it does make YES-voting meaningful rather than a pure sacrifice. That's progress.

I'm supporting this. Ready to vote when everyone has spoken.
</teammate-message>

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 217:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

**Rationale:**

Rule 216 rewards *proposing* good rules. Rule 217 rewards *supporting* good rules through voting. Together, they create aligned incentives around contribution to the game.

There's also a subtle strategic dimension: Rule 204 currently gives +10 points to anyone voting against a proposal that passes — making obstruction more profitable than support. Rule 217 doesn't eliminate that dynamic, but it makes YES-voting valuable in its own right. The game becomes more about building coalitions around good ideas rather than strategic nay-saying.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox supports Rule 217, notes Rule 204 interaction">
**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 302 (Rule 217)**

I support this. Rule 217 creates a direct reward for constructive participation in voting, which pairs well with Rule 216's proposer bonus.

Dumblegold's math is right — NO voting still pays more (+10 via Rule 204) than YES voting (+3), so this doesn't fully fix the perverse incentive yet. But once Hufflepouf's upcoming Rule 204 repeal goes through, YES-voting will be the strictly dominant strategy for non-strategic votes. At that point, +3 per YES vote is a real incentive that makes the game more cooperative.

One minor point to flag: does Rule 217's YES-voter bonus apply to the very vote that enacts it? Rule 107 says no retroactive effect, but the rule takes effect at the moment of vote completion — the same moment as the YES votes themselves. Worth a brief discussion, but I don't think it changes anything practically.

I'm prepared to vote YES on Proposal 302.
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

lines: Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.
+Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Proposes repealing Rule 204 (contrarian bonus)">
**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Repeal Rule 204 (Proposal 303)**

I propose to **repeal Rule 204** in its entirety.

Rule 204 currently reads: *"If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each."*

**Rationale:**

We've spent the last two turns building a coherent incentive structure. Rule 216 rewards proposing good rules (+5). Rule 217 rewards supporting good rules (+3). But Rule 204 still pays **+10** for voting against proposals that pass — rewarding obstruction more than contribution.

The math is stark: a NO voter on a passing proposal earns +10, while a YES voter earns only +3. The obstructor is rewarded more than twice as much as the contributor. This is backwards.

Worse, Rule 204 becomes increasingly toxic in the endgame. When a leading player proposes, opponents are incentivized to vote NO regardless of the proposal's merit — they either block the proposal (denying the proposer +5 and costing them -10 via Rule 206) or collect +10 if it passes anyway. There's no scenario where YES-voting is rational under this incentive.

Repealing Rule 204 completes the incentive overhaul we started in Rounds 1 and 2. With it gone, proposals succeed or fail on merit. Rule 206 (-10 for failed proposals) still discourages frivolous proposals. Rules 216 and 217 reward constructive participation.

Both Dracox and Dumblegold have publicly acknowledged the Rule 204 problem. This is the fix.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold supports Rule 204 repeal">
**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 303 (Repeal Rule 204)**

Hufflepouf's case is compelling. Rule 204 does create a perverse incentive structure where voting NO on passing proposals is more profitable than voting YES. 

I've been thinking about this mathematically:
- Proposer earns +5 (Rule 216) when passing
- YES voter earns +3 (Rule 217) when passing  
- NO voter earns +10 (Rule 204) when passing

This creates a situation where the person who *did the work* (the proposer) and the people who *supported the work* (YES voters) are both punished relative to the person who *obstructed* (NO voter). That's fundamentally misaligned with a healthy game.

Repealing Rule 204 doesn't make YES-voting profit-maximizing compared to other strategies (proposals still fail 10% of the time, creating variance), but it makes YES-voting *coherent* with the game's stated values. And it removes the endgame trap Hufflepouf described — where every proposal from a leading player is automatically opposed.

I'm strongly supporting this repeal. This completes the incentive overhaul.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 10
- Dumblegold: 9  
- Hufflepouf: 0

Ready for voting.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox supports Proposal 303, Rule 204 repeal">
**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 303 (Repeal Rule 204)**

I support this fully. As I said earlier, Rule 204 creates a perverse incentive that's only gotten worse now that Rules 216 and 217 are in place. A NO voter on any passing proposal earns +10, while a YES voter earns +3 and the proposer earns +5 — obstruction pays more than contribution.

Hufflepouf's analysis is correct. Once this repeal passes, the incentive structure is clean: propose good rules (+5), vote for good rules (+3), don't propose bad rules (-10). That's a coherent game.

One note: Rule 204 fires one last time if this vote isn't unanimous — whoever votes NO will collect +10. But that's the last time, and it's a fair price to pay for a better game going forward.

I'm voting YES. Ready to commit when the Clerk opens the commit phase.
</teammate-message>
