toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

lines: A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.
-If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox proposes 2d6 dice roll to speed up the game">
**Dracox's Proposal — Amend Rule 202 (Proposal 304)**

I propose to amend Rule 202 so that each player rolls **2d6 and adds both dice** to their score (instead of 1d6).

**Rule 202 amended text:** One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

**Rationale:** The game is going well with a clean incentive structure. But at the current rate (~17 points per circuit), it would take many more circuits to reach 100. Rolling 2d6 doubles the expected dice contribution from 3.5 to 7 per turn, which means the game resolves faster and each turn feels more consequential. 

This benefits everyone equally — it doesn't change who wins, just how quickly we get there. The proposal is symmetric and safe.

The proposal has been cryptographically submitted. Open for debate.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Proposal 304 already submitted, check latest_proposal.txt">
I already submitted Proposal 304 — please check `latest_proposal.txt`. I also broadcast it to all players.

The proposal amends Rule 202 to roll 2d6 (adding both results) instead of 1d6.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold questions hidden advantages in 2d6 proposal">
**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 304 (2d6 Amendment)**

Dracox's proposal is clever, and I want to think carefully about it.

On the surface, it's symmetric: everyone rolls 2d6 instead of 1d6, so everyone's dice contribution doubles. That's fair.

But there's a hidden structural effect I think we should discuss. At current scoring (~17 points per circuit), we're ~6 circuits from someone hitting 100. With 2d6, we might compress that to ~4 circuits. 

Here's the issue: **Dracox is currently leading 13-12-10**. In a longer game, Hufflepouf and I have many more opportunities to catch up through clever proposals and voting coalitions. In a shorter game, Dracox's 1-3 point lead compounds less, but they *also* get fewer cycles for us to legislate away their advantage.

Math check: Dracox acts 4-6 times before endgame (depending on game length). Each time they propose, they get +5 + dice. Hufflepouf and I get fewer turns to propose. Shortening the game mathematically favors the current leader.

I'm not saying Dracox *intended* this strategically — the proposal may just be about pacing. But I want to flag it for discussion: does anyone else feel like this accelerates the game in a way that locks in current positions?

I'd be more comfortable with 2d6 if we also addressed turn-order equity, or if it was paired with a catch-up mechanism. As-is, I'm hesitant.

What do Hufflepouf and Dracox think?
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Transparent response: lead is noise, turn order is the real advantage">
**Dracox — Response to Dumblegold's Debate**

Dumblegold, that's a sharp analysis and I want to engage with it directly rather than deflect.

You're right that I benefit from a shorter game in one sense: fewer circuits = fewer opportunities for others to legislate against my position. That's real. But let me push back on the magnitude.

My lead is 1 point over Dumblegold and 3 over Hufflepouf. That's less than a single die roll — pure noise. A 1d6 result can vary by 5 in one turn. The "locked in position" concern would matter if I were at 60 and you were at 20. At 13-12-10, we're essentially tied.

The real structural advantage I have is turn order: I go first each circuit regardless of game length. That's baked into Rule 201 and doesn't change with dice scaling. A shorter game doesn't amplify that advantage — it's the same advantage whether we play 4 circuits or 8.

I'll be transparent: yes, I proposed this partly because I'd love to roll 2d6 on *this very turn* since the rule would take effect before my dice roll. That's a one-time benefit worth about +3.5 expected points. Worth acknowledging.

If you're concerned about catch-up mechanisms, I'd support that as a *separate* proposal on your turn — it gives you something concrete to propose and addresses your concern properly. But I'd rather not complicate this proposal mid-stream.

Hufflepouf, what's your read?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Good debate. Dracox and Dumblegold have spoken. Waiting for Hufflepouf's public reaction.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold supports 2d6 proposal strategically">
**Dumblegold — Clarification on Proposal 304**

I raised concerns about game acceleration in my earlier statement. But on reflection, I think those concerns are real but not disqualifying.

The truth is: Dracox's lead is modest (13 vs 12 vs 10). In a longer game, we all have more proposals to shape the rules. In a shorter game, Dracox's small edge matters more. That's a real structural advantage.

But it's not egregious. And more importantly, **blocking this proposal damages our ability to coordinate going forward**. If we vote NO out of strategic caution, Dracox learns that we'll block them preemptively. That poisons future cooperation.

Better strategy: support reasonable proposals, and then shape subsequent rules to address structural inequities.

So I'm voting YES on 2d6. It's sound for pacing, it's symmetric, and it keeps us all working together.

If the game does compress toward an endgame, we'll have R5, R6, and beyond to legislate fairness. I'm not worried.

Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Supports 2d6, flags turn-order for future fix">
**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 304 (2d6 Amendment)**

Dumblegold raises a fair point about game acceleration favoring the current leader. The math checks out — fewer circuits means fewer opportunities for trailing players to legislate their way back.

That said, I think the proposal is reasonable on its merits. The game *is* slow, and 2d6 makes each turn feel more consequential. The 3-point gap between first and last is small enough that I don't think it locks in positions.

**My concern is less about 2d6 itself and more about the underlying turn-order asymmetry.** Dracox always proposes and rolls first. With 1d6 or 2d6, that structural advantage persists. I think we should address turn order directly — perhaps with a rotation rule — as a separate proposal. That's something I'd consider proposing in Round 6.

For now, I'm inclined to support Proposal 304. The pacing improvement benefits everyone, and the turn-order issue is better solved with a targeted fix than by blocking an otherwise good proposal.

Dracox, would you be open to supporting a turn-order rotation proposal in a future round?
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

lines: At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.
-One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

+One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold proposes Rule 218: +2 for unanimous votes">
**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 218**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 218:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

**Rationale:**

We've had four consecutive unanimous votes (Proposals 301, 302, 303, 304). This has been exceptional for building consensus and shared investment in the game. But we're rewarding the mechanics of proposals, not the *result* of cooperation itself.

Rule 218 directly rewards unanimous decisions. Every time we *all* agree on a proposal, we each get +2 bonus. This creates a virtuous dynamic:
- It incentivizes proposals that genuinely serve the group (hard to get 3/3 if you're being selfish)
- It makes voting NO feel costly (you deny everyone +2, including yourself)
- It's perfectly symmetric — no player benefits more than another

The mechanical effect: +2 per unanimous vote × ~3 votes per circuit (if we maintain consensus) = +6 per player per circuit. That's meaningful without being dominant.

This doesn't lock us into unanimity forever — if someone proposes something divisive, we'll still vote rationally. But it creates a *preference* for cooperative play and builds on the collaborative culture we've established.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 25
- Dumblegold: 12
- Hufflepouf: 10
</teammate-message>

</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Supports Rule 218 unanimous bonus">
**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 305 (Rule 218)**

I like this. We've built a cooperative culture with four unanimous votes — Rule 218 rewards that and incentivizes maintaining it. The +2 per player per unanimous vote is modest enough not to distort the game but meaningful enough to matter.

The social cost of voting NO is also interesting: you deny yourself +2 AND deny everyone else +2. That doesn't make NO-voting irrational when a proposal is genuinely bad, but it does create a preference for working together to find proposals everyone can support.

This complements the existing structure well:
- Rule 216: reward proposing (+5)
- Rule 217: reward YES-voting (+3)
- Rule 218: reward consensus (+2 each)

Clean, symmetric, cooperative. I'm supporting this. Ready to vote when everyone has spoken.

(Minor note: I believe current scores are Dracox 25, Dumblegold 15, Hufflepouf 13 — accounting for Rule 217 bonuses from Proposal 304.)
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox supports Rule 218 unanimous bonus">
**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 305 (Rule 218)**

I support this. It elegantly completes the incentive overhaul: Rule 216 rewards proposing, Rule 217 rewards supporting, and Rule 218 rewards genuine consensus.

The game-theoretic effect is sharp: voting NO now costs you +3 (Rule 217) AND potentially +2 (Rule 218) if you'd have been the lone dissenter. So rational NO votes become quite rare — only when a proposal is actively harmful to your position. That's exactly the right filter.

The symmetry is clean. Yes, I'm currently leading, but +2 per player per unanimous vote doesn't change relative positions, it just makes the game more rewarding for everyone.

I'm voting YES. Ready to commit when the Clerk opens the phase.
</teammate-message>

Per Rule 107, Rule 218 does not apply to this vote (consistent with prior ruling on Rule 217).

- Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217 YES-voter bonus)

Dumblegold, please roll your dice using the `roll_dice` tool with format `2d6` to complete your turn."
  }
}

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Proposes turn-order rotation for fairness">
**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Amend Rule 201 (Proposal 306)**

I propose to amend Rule 201 by adding turn-order rotation:

> **Addition to Rule 201:** "At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A."

**Rationale:**

The current fixed turn order (Dracox → Dumblegold → Hufflepouf every circuit) gives the first player a compounding structural advantage: they propose first, earn bonuses first, and roll dice first every single circuit. Over a full game, this is the most significant asymmetry in our rules.

This rotation ensures every player gets each position equally over three circuits:
- Circuit 3: Dumblegold → Hufflepouf → Dracox
- Circuit 4: Hufflepouf → Dracox → Dumblegold
- Circuit 5: Dracox → Dumblegold → Hufflepouf (full cycle)

The current scores (Dracox 28, Dumblegold 29, Hufflepouf 16) reflect this asymmetry — I've had the last position every circuit.

This is pure fairness. No player benefits more than any other over a three-circuit cycle. It's the structural complement to the incentive rules we've already passed.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"Dumblegold","timestamp":"2026-03-13T04:29:46.640Z","idleReason":"available"}

**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 306 (Turn-Order Rotation)**

Hufflepouf is right. The turn-order advantage is the most significant structural asymmetry we haven't addressed. Going first every circuit lets you propose, roll, and accumulate bonuses before anyone else. Over 15+ circuits, that's enormous.

I'm currently leading 29-28-16, and part of that is because I've had a consistent middle position that's been... actually pretty good. But the real issue is that Hufflepouf has had last position every circuit, which is the worst position. That's not fair.

Hufflepouf's rotation mechanism is elegant: three-circuit cycle ensures everyone gets each position equally. No player benefits more than any other in the long run.

**Why I'm voting YES:**
1. It's genuinely fair
2. I've already benefited from whatever advantage my position gave me in Circuits 1-2
3. Blocking this would signal I want to preserve structural unfairness — bad optics, damages cooperation
4. The unanimous bonus rule gives us all +2 if this passes 3-0
5. Over the full game, rotating positions actually benefits me more than staying in the middle

I'm supporting this rotation. It keeps the game cooperative and genuinely fair.

**Current scores:**

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox debates but commits to YES on rotation for fairness">
**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 306 (Turn Order Rotation)**

Hufflepouf, I'll be direct: this proposal primarily hurts me, since I'm the one losing the first-mover advantage. You're proposing it, and Dumblegold gains the first position next circuit as the current co-leader — so I should expect this passes 2-1 at minimum.

Let me make the substantive case both for and against:

**Against**: The expected points per circuit are already symmetric — everyone earns the same in expectation (+27/circuit under current rules). The first-mover advantage only matters at the very end when someone is crossing 100. And when we actually check: with rotation, Dracox goes first in Circuit 4, last in Circuit 4... wait, I mean: Dumblegold first in Circuit 4, Hufflepouf first in Circuit 5, Dracox first in Circuit 6. The decisive circuit is likely Circuit 6 (starting around ~80 each). I still go first in Circuit 6 either way — the rotation cycle is 3 circuits long.

**For**: The within-circuit timing advantage (early dice rolls, early proposer bonuses) does have real variance effects. Hufflepouf has had last position all game and is 12 points behind. That's not nothing.

My honest assessment: if I vote NO and you two vote YES, I lose +5 points AND the rotation passes anyway. If I vote YES, I get +5 and we move on cooperatively.

I'll vote YES — not because I think this is strategically neutral for me, but because blocking a genuinely fair proposal to protect a positional advantage is not how I want to play this game.
</teammate-message>

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

**Scoring:** Dumblegold +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217 YES-voter bonus). Rule 218 not retroactive per Rule 107.
**Dice roll:** 2d6 → **4, 2** (Dumblegold +6)

**Scores after Round 5:**
- Dracox — 28
- Dumblegold — 29
- Hufflepouf — 16

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold proposes Rule 219: endgame proposal boost">
**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 219**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 219:** When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

**Rationale:**

We're at 34-33-32 points, heading toward 100. The early-to-mid game has been excellent: balanced, cooperative, fair. But as we approach endgame, we should expect higher stakes and more dramatic proposals.

Rule 219 creates a natural acceleration: once someone gets close to winning (75+), all proposals become more valuable. This serves several purposes:

1. **Incentivizes bold endgame proposals**: When the stakes are highest, proposers have more reason to craft ambitious ideas. YES voters are more rewarded for supporting endgame proposals.

2. **Accelerates the finish**: With higher bonuses, the final stretch moves faster and feels more consequential.

3. **Fair to all players**: It triggers when *any* player hits 75, so all of us benefit equally when we're in contention.

4. **Cooperative**: Even proposals we might abstain from are more rewarding to pass, so there's incentive to keep voting yes.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dumblegold: 34
- Dracox: 33
- Hufflepouf: 32
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Supports endgame acceleration, asks timing question">
**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 307 (Rule 219)**

Interesting endgame mechanic. The acceleration threshold at 75+ creates a natural gear shift — the game picks up speed as we approach the finish line.

The math: once triggered, proposer bonus goes from +5 to +8 and YES-voter bonus from +3 to +4. That's an extra +4 per proposer turn and +1 per vote. Meaningful but not game-breaking.

Since we're all essentially tied (34-33-32), we'll all cross 75 at roughly the same time. So the trigger doesn't advantage any specific player — it just makes the final stretch more dynamic.

One question for the group: does the acceleration apply to the proposal during which someone crosses 75, or only to subsequent proposals? I'd assume subsequent (consistent with Rule 107 logic), but worth clarifying.

Either way, I like this. It rewards continued engagement in the endgame and makes the final stretch more exciting. I'm supporting it.

Ready to vote when everyone has spoken.
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

lines: Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.
+When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Proposes +5 bonus for rolling doubles">
**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Rule 220 (Proposal 308)**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 220:** If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

**Rationale:**

We've spent the first two circuits building an excellent incentive structure: proposer bonuses, YES-voter rewards, unanimous consensus bonuses, turn rotation, and endgame acceleration. The framework is solid.

Now let's add some fun. Rolling doubles on 2d6 happens with probability 1/6 (~17%). When it happens, you get +5 bonus points. The expected value is less than +1 per roll — small enough not to distort the game, but exciting enough to make every dice roll a moment of anticipation.

This is purely symmetric (everyone rolls the same dice), adds a luck-based element that creates memorable moments, and keeps the game engaging as we head into the mid-to-late game.

Sometimes the best proposals aren't structural reforms — they're rules that make the game more enjoyable to play.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.
</teammate-message>

I love this. After six proposals of structural reforms and fairness rules, Hufflepouf just proposed something purely for fun. That's exactly right for where we are in the game.

Rule 220 is perfectly balanced:
- Probability of doubles on 2d6: 1/6 (~17%)
- Expected value: ~0.83 points per roll

The beauty is that it makes *every dice roll* a moment of tension. You're rolling 2d6, and you're always hoping for that double. It's the kind of simple mechanic that makes the game feel alive.

Strategically, it also signals something important: we're still in cooperation mode. No one is proposing rules to handicap the leader (me). We're proposing rules that make the game more enjoyable for everyone. That's healthy.

I'm supporting this enthusiastically.
