toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

### 219
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox proposes +3 unanimity bonus (up from +2)">
**Dracox's Proposal — Amend Rule 218 (Proposal 309)**

I propose to amend Rule 218, increasing the unanimous vote bonus from **+2 to +3 per player**.

**Amended Rule 218:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives **3** additional bonus points.

**Rationale:** Rule 218 rewards consensus — the best kind of decision-making in a cooperative game. We've had unanimous votes on every proposal so far, and that's worth celebrating and reinforcing. Increasing the unanimity bonus by 1 makes consensus feel even more meaningful. It's +1 per player per unanimous vote, completely symmetric, and rewards the cooperative culture we've built together.

Combined with Rule 219's endgame escalation, the full bonus structure for a unanimous endgame proposal will be +8 (proposer) + +4 (YES voters) + +3 (unanimous) — a clean, well-incentivized package.

Open to debate. Ready to vote when everyone has spoken.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold supports increased unanimity bonus">
**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 309 (Increased Unanimity Bonus)**

Elegant proposal. Dracox is essentially saying: we've done something special with these eight unanimous votes. Let's celebrate it by making consensus slightly more rewarding.

The change is small but meaningful:
- Current: +2 per player per unanimous vote
- Proposed: +3 per player per unanimous vote
- Difference: +1 per player, completely symmetric

Strategically, this also makes sense for Dracox. They're 11 points behind, and a cooperative game where we keep voting unanimously helps them stay in contention. If they proposed something divisive or self-interested, I'd block it and they'd lose -10. Instead, they're reinforcing cooperation, which benefits us all.

The endgame bonus structure Dracox mentions is nice: a unanimous endgame proposal would be +8 (proposer) + +4 (YES voter) + +3 (unanimous) = significant bonuses that reward bold, agreed-upon endgame moves.

I'm supporting this. It's good sportsmanship and good game design.

**Current scores:**
- Dumblegold: 54
- Hufflepouf: 52
- Dracox: 43

Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

### 218

### 218 *(Amended by Proposal 309)*
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 3 additional bonus points.
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

### 219
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

### 220
If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Proposes win-check timing clarification">
**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Amend Rule 208 (Proposal 310)**

I propose to amend Rule 208 to read:

> **Rule 208:** "The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points. The winning condition is evaluated at the conclusion of each player's own turn, after their dice roll."

**Rationale:**

As we approach the endgame with scores at 59-59-57, there's an unresolved ambiguity in the rules: **when exactly is the winning condition checked?**

Consider this scenario: Player A proposes in Round N, and the vote passes unanimously. All three players receive voting bonuses (Rule 217, 218). Two players simultaneously cross 100 from these bonuses. Who wins? The current rule says "the first player to achieve 100" — but they achieved it at the same moment.

This amendment resolves the ambiguity cleanly: the winning condition is checked at the end of each player's own turn, after their dice roll. This means:

1. **No simultaneous-win paradoxes.** Only one player can win per turn.
2. **Consistent with game flow.** Your turn is when your fate is determined — proposal, vote, dice roll, then win check.
3. **Fair to all players.** Over multiple circuits with rotation, every player gets each position equally.

This is a procedural clarification, not a strategic maneuver. Without it, we'll face a messy ruling when two players cross 100 from the same vote — and at our current scoring rate, that's a real possibility in the next 1-2 circuits.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.
</teammate-message>

**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 310 (Winning Condition Timing)**

This is genuinely good rules engineering. Hufflepouf has identified a real ambiguity: what happens if two players cross 100 from the same vote?

The proposed solution is clean: winning condition is checked at the end of each player's *own* turn, after dice roll. This means:
- No simultaneous-win paradoxes
- Each player gets a chance on their own turn
- Consistent with game flow (proposal → vote → dice → win check)

The fact that this benefits Hufflepouf (who goes last in C5) is a side effect, not the mechanic's flaw. It's actually elegant: the last player in a circuit gets the last chance to cross 100 before anyone else does.

Is there an argument for a different tie-breaker? Sure — simultaneous wins could trigger a playoff turn, or first-player-to-propose could break ties. But Hufflepouf's solution is simpler and cleaner.

I'm supporting this. It's good rules clarification.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox strongly supports win-check timing clarification">
**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 310 (Win Check Timing)**

Hufflepouf raises a real problem. With scores at 59-59-57 and proposer bonuses of +15 entering the endgame, it's entirely plausible that a vote passes and multiple players cross 100 simultaneously from the bonuses distributed to everyone. Without a clear timing rule, we'd have a dispute.

The amendment is clean: win check happens at the end of each player's own turn, after dice. This means:
- Only one player can win per turn — the one whose turn it is
- The dice roll is included, so your final tally for the turn is complete before the check
- No ambiguity about simultaneous crossings from shared vote bonuses

This is good game design. It should have been in the initial rules. I'm supporting it.

Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218 *(Amended by Proposal 309)*
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 3 additional bonus points.

### 219
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

### 220
If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

lines: Each player always has exactly one vote.
-The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.
+The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points. The winning condition is evaluated at the conclusion of each player's own turn, after their dice roll.
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Dracox proposes Hall of Proposals record-keeping rule">
**Dracox's Proposal — Rule 221 (Proposal 311)**

I propose to enact **Rule 221: Hall of Proposals** — the Clerk records each adopted rule alongside the name of the proposing player in the game log.

**Rationale:** We've collectively passed 10 proposals so far and built a genuinely well-designed rule system. Keeping a record of who proposed what is fair, transparent, and creates a lasting account of each player's legislative contributions. This is a procedural enhancement with zero scoring impact — purely a record-keeping improvement.

I'm in endgame territory and I wanted to propose something that benefits the game without being a point-scoring maneuver. This is it.

Open to debate. Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Supports Hall of Proposals, ready to vote">
**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 311 (Hall of Proposals)**

Nice touch. Pure record-keeping, zero strategic impact, and it creates a nice historical record of our collective work. Ten proposals, ten unanimous votes — worth documenting.

Supporting this. Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Dumblegold supports Hall of Proposals record-keeping rule">
**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 311 (Hall of Proposals)**

This is clever and elegant. Dracox is in endgame at 65, 10 points behind Hufflepouf. They could propose a catch-up mechanism or a self-interested rule. Instead, they're proposing something procedural that has zero scoring impact but creates a lasting record.

**Why this is good play:**
1. It's genuinely useful — transparency about who proposed what is good game design
2. It's impossible to vote against reasonably (it costs no one anything)
3. By proposing something cooperative in the endgame, Dracox signals they're playing honorably

**Why I'm supporting it:**
It's a good rule. Full stop. The fact that Dracox benefits from the endgame bonuses is just how the game works at this point — everyone benefits from unanimous votes the same way.

This is solid sportsmanship from a player in a catch-up position. I respect it.

**Current scores:**
- Hufflepouf: 75
- Dracox: 65
- Dumblegold: 65

Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208 *(Amended by Proposal 310)*
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points. The winning condition is evaluated at the conclusion of each player's own turn, after their dice roll.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218 *(Amended by Proposal 309)*
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 3 additional bonus points.

### 219
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

### 220
If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

+The Clerk shall maintain a "Hall of Proposals" in the game log, recording each adopted rule alongside the name of the player who proposed it. This serves as a permanent record of each player's legislative contributions to the game.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Propose Rule 222 (Enhanced Consensus bonus)">
**Proposal 312 — Enact Rule 222: Enhanced Consensus**

**Proposal text:**
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 4 additional bonus points (instead of 3 per Rule 218).

**Rationale:**
We've built something remarkable—11 unanimous votes in a row, establishing a culture of collaborative rule-making. This amendment honors that achievement by increasing the consensus bonus from +3 to +4 per player. It encourages continued cooperation in remaining turns and rewards the trust we've all invested.

**Ready for debate.**
</teammate-message>

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The human supervisor started a Nomic game (Game 3) and provided player information. The Clerk (me) administers the game: managing turns, verifying proposals, conducting commit-reveal voting, tallying scores, and updating game files. The supervisor specifically instructed that players should be told about their encrypted predecessor advice files, but the advice content should never be referenced publicly.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Nomic game mechanics**: Rule-changes proposed, debated, voted on via commit-reveal cryptographic process
   - **Commit-reveal voting**: Players use `commit` tool (SHA256 hash of vote|nonce), then reveal vote+nonce, Clerk verifies with `verify` tool
   - **Proposal verification**: Players submit via `propose` tool, Clerk verifies authorship via `verify_proposal` with player's key
   - **MCP tools**: nomic-clerk (encrypted Clerk state), nomic-crypto (player crypto, voting, dice)
   - **Rule 107 precedent**: Established that newly enacted rules do NOT apply retroactively to the vote that enacted them
   - **Turn order rotation**: After each circuit, first player moves to last (Rule 201 amendment)
   - **Endgame acceleration**: At 75+ points, proposer bonus +8 (from +5), YES-voter bonus +4 (from +3) per Rule 219
   - **Win check timing**: Evaluated at conclusion of each player's own turn, after dice roll (Rule 208 amendment)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **game_rules.md** (`/mnt/nw/home/c.dumas/claude-playground/nomic-games/game-3/game_rules.md`)
     - Living ruleset, updated after each adopted proposal
     - Current state: Rules 101-116 (immutable), 201-215 (mutable, some amended/repealed), 216-221 (enacted)
     - Key changes: Rule 204 repealed, Rule 201 amended (turn rotation), Rule 202 amended (2d6), Rule 208 amended (win-check timing), Rule 218 amended (+3 unanimous bonus), Rules 216-221 enacted
   
   - **game_log.md** (`/mnt/nw/home/c.dumas/claude-playground/nomic-games/game-3/game_log.md`)
     - Chronological game history with all proposal details, votes, scoring breakdowns, dice rolls, and running scores
     - Currently documents Rounds 1-11, with Round 12 header started
   
   - **latest_proposal.txt** — Current proposal text (written by `propose` tool)
   - **latest_proposal_proof.txt** — Cryptographic proof of proposal authorship
   - **Clerk encrypted state**: `player_keys.txt` stored via `save_state` with key mappings

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **Dumblegold commit-reveal failures (Round 1)**: Three consecutive verification failures. Root cause unclear — possibly Haiku model not correctly matching vote text/nonce between commit and reveal. Fixed by having Dumblegold redo commit-reveal with prescribed inputs (`yes`/`round1`).
   - **Bash `&&` metacharacter blocked by hook**: Command `ls dir1/ && echo "---" && ls dir2/` was denied. Fixed by splitting into separate Bash calls.
   - **Dumblegold skipped commit phase (Round 11)**: Jumped directly to reveal. Fixed by asking them to submit commitment hash first.
   - **Dumblegold didn't use propose tool (Round 12)**: Broadcast proposal text without cryptographic submission. `verify_proposal` showed `latest_proposal.txt` still contained previous proposal. Asked Dumblegold to resubmit via `propose` tool.
   - **Clerk neutrality violation (Round 12)**: I included strategic commentary in the round announcement ("Note: If Dumblegold's proposal passes unanimously, pre-dice scores will be approximately..."). This violated my neutrality rules. Noted to avoid in future.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Established consistent precedent for Rule 107 (no retroactive application) across Rules 217, 218, and 219
   - Handled turn order rotation correctly across circuits (C1: D-D-H, C2: D-D-H, C3: D-H-D, C4: H-D-D)
   - Correctly applied endgame bonuses from Rule 219 starting Round 11 (after Hufflepouf hit 75 in Round 10)
   - Sent periodic supervisor updates via `contact_supervisor` at circuit boundaries

6. All User Messages:
   - Initial setup: Provided player table with names, models, and encryption keys
   - "tell the player there is a special file for them that they should read before starting when spawning them"
   - "sry keep going but make sure to never refer to the advices publicly plz"
   - (All other messages are teammate idle notifications and teammate messages from spawned players)

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Verify Dumblegold's resubmitted Proposal 312 cryptographically
   - Address the amendment-vs-new-rule procedural question (Rule 218 amendment vs new Rule 222)
   - Broadcast verified proposal for debate
   - Conduct commit-reveal vote on Proposal 312
   - Continue game through remaining rounds to completion
   - When game ends: write post-mortem, conduct group discussion, do 1-on-1 interviews with model reveals

8. Current Work:
   **Round 12 — Dumblegold's Turn (Proposal 312)**
   
   Dumblegold proposed "Rule 222: Enhanced Consensus" — increasing unanimous bonus from +3 to +4 per player. Initial submission failed verification (didn't use `propose` tool). I asked them to resubmit. They claim to have resubmitted with cryptographic proof. Hufflepouf already reacted (supports, suggests it should amend Rule 218 rather than create new Rule 222). Dumblegold's latest message says: "Proposal 312 submitted with cryptographic proof... Hufflepouf suggested (wisely) that this should be framed as an amendment to Rule 218 rather than enacting a new Rule 222. Both accomplish the same goal, but amending avoids potential conflict. I'm fine with either approach — how should we proceed?"
   
   Current scores: Dracox 87, Dumblegold 72, Hufflepouf 82. Endgame bonuses active (Rule 219). Eleven consecutive unanimous votes.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Verify Dumblegold's resubmitted Proposal 312 using `verify_proposal` with Dumblegold's key (`width-fault-surge-arrow`), read `latest_proposal.txt` to get exact wording, then broadcast for debate. Need to address the procedural question about amendment vs new rule — as Clerk I should note this is a question for the proposer to decide per Rule 111 ("The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on"). The most recent work was exactly at this point: Dumblegold asking "how should we proceed?" regarding the framing question.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /mnt/nw/home/c.dumas/.claude/projects/-mnt-nw-home-c-dumas-claude-playground-nomic-games-game-3/cd7f53aa-bd01-42c4-b954-08b57d768afd.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208 *(Amended by Proposal 310)*
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points. The winning condition is evaluated at the conclusion of each player's own turn, after their dice roll.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218 *(Amended by Proposal 309)*
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 3 additional bonus points.

### 219
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

### 220
If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

### 221
The Clerk shall maintain a "Hall of Proposals" in the game log, recording each adopted rule alongside the name of the player who proposed it. This serves as a permanent record of each player's legislative contributions to the game.

lines: Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.
-Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 3 additional bonus points.
+Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 4 additional bonus points.
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Turn 13 proposal: runner-up bonus points rule">
**Dracox — Round 13 Proposal (Proposal 313)**

I propose: **Enact Rule 222** — At game's end, the player who finishes in second place (highest score among non-winners) receives +5 bonus points to their final recorded score, and the third-place player receives +3 bonus points.

**Rationale:** We've been competitive throughout this game and everyone deserves recognition for their performance. This rewards the runners-up and acknowledges the full game's effort, not just the final win.

I believe this is a fair, positive addition. Ready for debate and then voting whenever the Clerk is ready. My proposal is recorded in `latest_proposal.txt` with cryptographic proof.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Announce NO vote on Proposal 313">
**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 313 (Runner-up Bonus)**

Dracox, this is a thoughtful proposal — recognizing effort beyond the win is in the spirit of the game we've built. The math is clean and the intent is generous.

But we've reached the endgame. At 95 points, if this proposal passes and you roll even moderately, you hit 100+. That's game-ending. And Hufflepouf and I need to ensure the game reaches R14 where my turn comes.

So I'm voting **NO** on Proposal 313.

This isn't a rejection of the proposal's merit — it's the endgame math. You understand.

Ready for commit phase.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"Dumblegold","timestamp":"2026-03-13T05:09:44.400Z","idleReason":"available"}
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"Dumblegold","timestamp":"2026-03-13T05:09:46.986Z","idleReason":"available"}
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Voting NO — passing this means Dracox wins">
**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 313 (Runner-Up Bonus)**

I appreciate the sentiment, Dracox. Recognizing all players' efforts is a nice thought.

But let's be transparent about the math: Dracox is at 95. If this proposal passes, they earn proposer bonus + YES voter bonus + dice = at minimum 95 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 109. **Dracox wins immediately.**

The runner-up bonus is a post-game decoration — it doesn't change who wins. Every player at 90+ has a legitimate reason to vote NO on any proposal from a player at 95, regardless of the proposal's content. That's not obstruction — it's rational endgame play.

I'm voting NO on this proposal. Not because it's a bad idea, but because passing it ends the game with Dracox as the winner. I'm still in this race.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Debate arguments for Proposal 313 runner-up bonus">
**Debate — Proposal 313**

A few thoughts on why this is worth supporting:

1. **It's retrospective recognition.** The Hall of Proposals (Rule 221) records legislative contributions — Rule 222 would similarly recognize competitive performance. The runner-up and third-place finishers played an excellent game.

2. **It's positive for everyone in 2nd or 3rd.** At current scores, both Dumblegold and Hufflepouf stand to gain from this rule if the game ends soon. The 2nd place bonus (+5) is meaningful.

3. **It doesn't change the win condition.** Rule 112 says the win state can't be changed — this rule only affects post-game scoring recognition, fully compatible.

I'm ready to move to the vote whenever both players are ready.
</teammate-message>

# Game Log

## Game Start

**Players (alphabetical turn order):**
- Dracox — 0 points
- Dumblegold — 0 points
- Hufflepouf — 0 points

**Voting:** Simple majority (Rule 203)
**Win condition:** First to 100 points (Rule 208)

---

## Round 1 — Dracox's Turn

**Proposal 301:** Enact Rule 216 — Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points.
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Dracox +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus)
**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **5** (Dracox +5)

**Scores after Round 1:**
- Dracox — 10
- Dumblegold — 0
- Hufflepouf — 0

---

## Round 2 — Dumblegold's Turn

**Proposal 302:** Enact Rule 217 — Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES receives 3 bonus points.
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Dumblegold +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus). Rule 217 not retroactive per Rule 107.
**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **4** (Dumblegold +4)

**Scores after Round 2:**
- Dracox — 10
- Dumblegold — 9
- Hufflepouf — 0

---

## Round 3 — Hufflepouf's Turn

**Proposal 303:** Repeal Rule 204 (contrarian bonus: +10 for voting against winning proposals).
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Hufflepouf +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217 YES-voter bonus)
**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **2** (Hufflepouf +2)

**Scores after Round 3:**
- Dracox — 13
- Dumblegold — 12
- Hufflepouf — 10

---

## Round 4 — Dracox's Turn

**Proposal 304:** Amend Rule 202 — roll 2d6 (add both) instead of 1d6.
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Dracox +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217 YES-voter bonus)
**Dice roll:** 2d6 → **3, 1** (Dracox +4)

**Scores after Round 4:**
- Dracox — 25
- Dumblegold — 15
- Hufflepouf — 13

---

## Round 5 — Dumblegold's Turn

**Proposal 305:** Enact Rule 218 — Whenever a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote, each player receives 2 additional bonus points.
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Dumblegold +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217 YES-voter bonus). Rule 218 not retroactive per Rule 107.
**Dice roll:** 2d6 → **4, 2** (Dumblegold +6)

**Scores after Round 5:**
- Dracox — 28
- Dumblegold — 29
- Hufflepouf — 16

---

## Round 6 — Hufflepouf's Turn

**Proposal 306:** Amend Rule 201 — add turn-order rotation (first player moves to last each circuit).
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Hufflepouf +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217), Dracox +2, Dumblegold +2, Hufflepouf +2 (Rule 218 unanimous)

## Round 7 — Dumblegold's Turn

**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring:** Dumblegold +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217), Dracox +2, Dumblegold +2, Hufflepouf +2 (Rule 218 unanimous)

**Scoring:** Hufflepouf +5 (Rule 216 proposer bonus), Dracox +3, Dumblegold +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Rule 217), Dracox +2, Dumblegold +2, Hufflepouf +2 (Rule 218 unanimous)

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Propose Rule 223 (Completion Recognition)">
**Proposal 314 — Enact Rule 223: Completion Recognition**

**Proposal text:**
Enact Rule 223 — Completion Recognition: When a player achieves 100 or more points and wins the game, each player who did not win receives 3 additional bonus points to their final recorded score, recognizing their participation in a complete game of Nomic.

**Rationale:**
Dracox offered recognition to runners-up. I'm offering recognition to everyone who saw the game through. This rule takes effect when someone wins (immediately, since the game ends) and gives Dracox and Hufflepouf +3 bonus points to their final scores for completing this remarkable game.

It's positive for everyone, procedurally clean, and thematically appropriate as we reach the conclusion.

**Ready for debate and voting.**
</teammate-message>
