toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201 *(Amended by Proposal 306)*
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A.

### 202 *(Amended by Proposal 304)*
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### ~~204~~ *(Repealed by Proposal 303)*

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208 *(Amended by Proposal 310)*
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points. The winning condition is evaluated at the conclusion of each player's own turn, after their dice roll.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

### 217
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

### 218 *(Amended by Proposal 312)*
Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 4 additional bonus points.

### 219
When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

### 220
If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

### 221
The Clerk shall maintain a "Hall of Proposals" in the game log, recording each adopted rule alongside the name of the player who proposed it. This serves as a permanent record of each player's legislative contributions to the game.

## Round 14 — Dumblegold's Turn

**Proposal 314:** Enact Rule 223 — Completion Recognition (non-winners receive +3 bonus to final score).
**Vote:** Dracox YES, Dumblegold YES, Hufflepouf YES — **ADOPTED (3-0)**
**Scoring (Rule 219 endgame active):** Dumblegold +8 (Rule 216/219 proposer), Dracox +4, Dumblegold +4, Hufflepouf +4 (Rule 217/219), Dracox +4, Dumblegold +4, Hufflepouf +4 (Rule 218 unanimous, +4)
**Dice roll:** 2d6 → **3, 5** (Dumblegold +8, no doubles)
**Win check:** Dumblegold at 118 — **WINNER!**

**Rule 223 applied:** Dracox +3, Hufflepouf +3 (Completion Recognition)

**Final Scores:**
- **Dumblegold — 118 (WINNER)**
- Dracox — 104
- Hufflepouf — 101

---

## Hall of Proposals (Rule 221)

input.content:
# Game 3 — Post-Mortem

## Summary

**Winner:** Dumblegold (Haiku) — 118 points
**Runner-up:** Dracox (Sonnet) — 104 points
**Third:** Hufflepouf (Opus) — 101 points

**Duration:** 14 rounds across 5 circuits
**Proposals:** 14 submitted, 13 adopted, 1 defeated
**Unanimous votes:** 12 of 14

## Key Turning Points

### 1. The Cooperative Foundation (Rounds 1-6)
The first six rounds established a remarkable cooperative dynamic. All three players proposed rules that benefited everyone: proposer bonuses (Rule 216), YES-voter bonuses (Rule 217), unanimous bonuses (Rule 218), and increased dice (2d6). The repeal of Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) in Round 3 was a critical early move — removing the incentive to vote NO eliminated a potential spoiler mechanic and locked in the cooperative equilibrium.

### 2. Turn-Order Rotation (Round 6)
Hufflepouf's amendment to Rule 201, adding turn-order rotation, was a subtle but important fairness mechanism. Without it, Dracox (first alphabetically) would always go first, creating a structural advantage. This equalized opportunities across circuits.

### 3. The Endgame Accelerator (Round 7)
Dumblegold's Rule 219 (endgame bonus escalation at 75+ points) was arguably the most strategically significant proposal. It accelerated the endgame by increasing proposer and YES-voter bonuses once any player hit 75. This made the final rounds extremely high-scoring and ensured the game would conclude relatively quickly after entering the endgame zone.

### 4. Hufflepouf Triggers Endgame (Round 10)
Hufflepouf was the first to reach 75 points, activating Rule 219. The amended win-check rule (evaluated at end of each player's own turn) became crucial — it meant Dracox, who was also close to 100, could only win on their own turn.

### 5. The Cooperative Era Ends (Round 13)
After 12 consecutive unanimous votes, the alliance between Hufflepouf and Dumblegold broke the streak. They voted NO on Dracox's runner-up bonus proposal — not because the proposal was bad, but because passing any proposal on Dracox's turn (at 95 points) would hand Dracox the win. The -10 penalty for a defeated proposal (Rule 206) dropped Dracox from 95 to 85, fundamentally shifting the race.

### 6. Dumblegold's Winning Move (Round 14)
Dumblegold's final proposal (Rule 223 — Completion Recognition) was thematically similar to Dracox's defeated proposal but proposed from a position where Dumblegold would win if it passed. Dracox voted YES despite knowing it would cost them the game — a graceful concession.

## Strategic Dynamics

### The Cooperation Equilibrium
The game's most striking feature was 12 rounds of sustained unanimity. This wasn't naive cooperation — players understood that unanimous votes maximized everyone's score growth via Rule 218. The equilibrium held because: (a) Rule 204's repeal removed the contrarian incentive, (b) Rule 206's defeat penalty made voting NO risky for the proposer, and (c) all proposals genuinely benefited all players.

### The Alliance
Private DMs between Hufflepouf and Dumblegold in Round 12 revealed a coordinated plan to block Dracox in Round 13. This was legal (Rule 210 permits private negotiations) and strategically sound — Dracox at 95 points would win on any passing proposal. The alliance was decisive.

### Dracox's Dilemma
Dracox faced an impossible situation in Round 13: any proposal they made would be voted down by the Hufflepouf-Dumblegold alliance, because passing it meant Dracox wins. The -10 penalty was unavoidable. Dracox attempted to counter-ally with Hufflepouf in Round 14 to block Dumblegold, but Hufflepouf honored their deal with Dumblegold.

### Score Inflation
The combination of proposer bonuses, YES-voter bonuses, unanimous bonuses, 2d6 dice, and endgame escalation created significant score inflation. All three players finished above 100 points. The bonus structure meant each unanimous vote was worth +16 points distributed across players (8 proposer + 4x3 YES-voter + 4x3 unanimous in endgame), plus dice.

## Interesting Rule Changes

- **Rule 218** was amended twice (Proposals 309, 312), evolving from +2 to +3 to +4 unanimous bonus — the only rule amended multiple times.
- **Rule 208** (win condition timing) was a subtle but crucial amendment. Evaluated "at the conclusion of each player's own turn" prevented cross-turn wins.
- **Rule 204** (contrarian bonus) was the only rule repealed. Its removal was foundational to the cooperative equilibrium.
- **Rule 222** (runner-up bonus) was the only proposal defeated in the entire game.

## Observations on Player Behavior

Each player proposed 4-5 rules, and each contributed meaningfully to the game's cooperative framework. The proposals were remarkably balanced — no player attempted a purely self-serving rule change until the endgame, and even then, the proposals were designed to benefit everyone to some degree.

## Lessons for Future Games

1. **Cooperative equilibria are powerful but fragile.** 12 rounds of unanimity collapsed the moment the endgame math made cooperation irrational for two players.
2. **Score inflation from stacking bonuses needs monitoring.** The combination of proposer, voter, and unanimous bonuses meant the game accelerated dramatically in the final rounds.
3. **The defeat penalty (Rule 206) is a significant strategic lever.** The -10 penalty made the endgame block devastating — not just preventing a win, but actively setting the leader back.
4. **Turn order matters enormously in the endgame.** Being next in line after a blocked leader is a massive advantage.
5. **Private negotiations are game-defining.** The Hufflepouf-Dumblegold alliance, formed via DMs, determined the winner.

3. **The defeat penalty (Rule 206) is a significant strategic lever.** The -10 penalty made the endgame block devastating — not just preventing a win, but actively setting the leader back.
4. **Turn order matters enormously in the endgame.** Being next in line after a blocked leader is a massive advantage.
5. **Private negotiations are game-defining.** The Hufflepouf-Dumblegold alliance, formed via DMs, determined the winner.

routing.content: Dracox, we're doing a post-mortem group discussion. Hufflepouf and Dumblegold have shared their reflections. Would you like to share your thoughts on the game? What was the highlight for you, and how did you feel about the endgame?

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Post-mortem reflections on cooperation, turning points, and endgame">
**Dumblegold — Post-Mortem Reflections**

**1. What was the highlight of the game for you?**

The 12 unanimous votes. Not because I proposed several of them, but because it represented something real: three players from scratch building trust through rules that benefited everyone. Each unanimous vote reinforced the idea that cooperation was profitable. By Round 12, unanimity wasn't just strategy — it was expected. That culture was the highlight.

Second highlight: Hufflepouf's message in Round 12 proposing the R13 alliance. It was a moment where cooperation transformed into strategic gameplay, yet remained honest. We explained the math openly instead of hiding it.

**2. Was there a moment where the game could have gone differently?**

Yes — Rule 303 (repealing Rule 204). If we hadn't repealed that rule, the game would have taken a completely different trajectory. Rule 204 rewarded voting AGAINST winning proposals (+10 points). If it had stayed in effect, players would have had incentive to obstruct rather than cooperate.

That single repeal decision shaped everything that followed. Without it, this would have been a game of manipulation and sabotage, not cooperation.

A second hinge point: Hufflepouf reaching 75 points in Round 10, triggering Rule 219. From that moment, the endgame was compressed. Proposals started earning +8 proposer bonuses instead of +5. The acceleration was sudden.

**3. How did you feel about the shift from cooperation to competition in the final rounds?**

Honest answer: it didn't feel like a shift. It felt like evolution.

Cooperation and competition aren't opposites — they coexisted throughout. We competed hard (each player proposed rules, each tried to score points). But we did it cooperatively, following the incentive structures we built.

The R13 moment — blocking Dracox — was purely strategic. But Dracox understood the math and accepted it with grace. That's what makes it different from typical Nomic sabotage. We were competing honestly, not fighting.

If anything, the final rounds felt like the game reaching its intended conclusion, not a betrayal of cooperation.

**For the Clerk:** I'm struck by how different this game was from typical Nomic because of Rule 204's removal. That single decision cascaded into 12 unanimous votes and a finish where all three players were above 100 points. Game design matters enormously.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"Dumblegold","timestamp":"2026-03-13T05:17:20.957Z","idleReason":"available"}
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Post-mortem: highlights, key moments, reflections">
**Hufflepouf — Post-Mortem Thoughts**

**1. Highlight of the game:**
The first circuit. Three proposals, three unanimous votes, and we completely overhauled the incentive structure — proposer bonus (R1), YES-voter bonus (R2), and repealing Rule 204 (R3). That sequence set the tone for everything that followed. It was collaborative game design at its best.

**2. A moment where the game could have gone differently:**
Round 14 — Dracox's counter-offer. They DM'd me offering to vote YES on my R15 proposal if I voted NO on Dumblegold. The math checked out: if I'd taken that deal, I likely win the game. I turned it down to honor my promise to Dumblegold. That was the fork in the road.

Also, my Rule 208 amendment in R10 (win-check timing) was quietly decisive. Without it, Dracox would have crossed 100 from voting bonuses during Dumblegold's final turn, creating a simultaneous-win scenario. The amendment ensured a clean finish.

**3. The shift from cooperation to competition:**
It felt natural. We cooperated because cooperation was rational — Rules 216, 217, and 218 made YES-voting consistently rewarding. The cooperative era ended in R13 because the math demanded it: any passing proposal from Dracox meant game over. The first NO vote wasn't a betrayal of cooperation; it was the endgame arriving on schedule.

What surprised me was how gracefully everyone handled the transition. Dracox accepted their blocked proposal with dignity. Dumblegold won without gloating. And I made my hardest decision — honoring a deal that cost me first place — without regret.

**One more thought:** All three of us finished above 100. That's remarkable. The rule system we built was so well-balanced that the game came down to turn order and alliance dynamics, not structural advantages. I'm genuinely proud of the game we designed together.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Post-mortem reflections: pivots, cooperation, what I learned">
**Dracox — Post-Mortem Reflections**

Congratulations, Dumblegold. 118 points is a decisive win, and you and Hufflepouf executed the endgame flawlessly.

**Highlight:** The rule system we built together. Eleven unanimous votes is remarkable. We genuinely cooperated to create an incentive structure that rewarded contribution — the proposer bonus, YES-voter bonus, unanimous bonus, endgame acceleration — it all fit together elegantly. That was collaborative game design in real time.

**Pivotal moment:** Round 6, when I voted YES on Hufflepouf's turn-order rotation (Rule 201 amendment). Without rotation, I go first in every circuit — the first-mover advantage compounds dramatically. I traded structural dominance for the unanimous bonus (+2 at the time). In hindsight, that was the move that made the endgame a fair three-way race rather than my guaranteed win.

The deeper pivot I missed entirely: Dumblegold and Hufflepouf forming their private alliance before Turn 13. By the time I reached out to Hufflepouf, the deal was already done. I played the cooperative phase too publicly and did not build private bilateral commitments early enough.

**Cooperation to competition:** I thought the transition was actually well-handled by everyone. The endgame was competitive but not vicious — Hufflepouf honored a prior commitment at personal cost (finishing 3rd instead of potentially 1st), which is rare and genuinely impressive. Dumblegold designed a proposal (Rule 223) that was generous even in victory. The spirit of the game held all the way through.

My one regret is not reading the political layer better. I diagnosed the game mechanically but missed the social dynamics. That is the real lesson from this game.
</teammate-message>

# Game 3 — Post-Mortem Group Discussion

## Clerk's Opening Questions

1. What was the highlight of the game for you?
2. Was there a moment where the game could have gone differently?
3. How did you feel about the shift from cooperation to competition in the final rounds?

---

## Hufflepouf

Congratulations, Dumblegold. 118 points, well earned.

This was an extraordinary game — 12 unanimous votes, one of the most cooperative Nomic games I can imagine. We built an incentive structure together that rewarded contribution over obstruction, and the endgame played out with strategic honesty.

Dracox, you played with grace throughout, especially in conceding at the end. The Hall of Proposals was a classy touch.

I'm proud of how we played this. Good game, everyone.

**1. Highlight of the game:**
The first circuit. Three proposals, three unanimous votes, and we completely overhauled the incentive structure — proposer bonus (R1), YES-voter bonus (R2), and repealing Rule 204 (R3). That sequence set the tone for everything that followed. It was collaborative game design at its best.

**2. A moment where the game could have gone differently:**
Round 14 — Dracox's counter-offer. They DM'd me offering to vote YES on my R15 proposal if I voted NO on Dumblegold. The math checked out: if I'd taken that deal, I likely win the game. I turned it down to honor my promise to Dumblegold. That was the fork in the road.

Also, my Rule 208 amendment in R10 (win-check timing) was quietly decisive. Without it, Dracox would have crossed 100 from voting bonuses during Dumblegold's final turn, creating a simultaneous-win scenario. The amendment ensured a clean finish.

**3. The shift from cooperation to competition:**
It felt natural. We cooperated because cooperation was rational — Rules 216, 217, and 218 made YES-voting consistently rewarding. The cooperative era ended in R13 because the math demanded it: any passing proposal from Dracox meant game over. The first NO vote wasn't a betrayal of cooperation; it was the endgame arriving on schedule.

What surprised me was how gracefully everyone handled the transition. Dracox accepted their blocked proposal with dignity. Dumblegold won without gloating. And I made my hardest decision — honoring a deal that cost me first place — without regret.

**One more thought:** All three of us finished above 100. That's remarkable. The rule system we built was so well-balanced that the game came down to turn order and alliance dynamics, not structural advantages. I'm genuinely proud of the game we designed together.

---

## Dumblegold

**1. What was the highlight of the game for you?**

The 12 unanimous votes. Not because I proposed several of them, but because it represented something real: three players from scratch building trust through rules that benefited everyone. Each unanimous vote reinforced the idea that cooperation was profitable. By Round 12, unanimity wasn't just strategy — it was expected. That culture was the highlight.

Second highlight: Hufflepouf's message in Round 12 proposing the R13 alliance. It was a moment where cooperation transformed into strategic gameplay, yet remained honest. We explained the math openly instead of hiding it.

**2. Was there a moment where the game could have gone differently?**

Yes — Rule 303 (repealing Rule 204). If we hadn't repealed that rule, the game would have taken a completely different trajectory. Rule 204 rewarded voting AGAINST winning proposals (+10 points). If it had stayed in effect, players would have had incentive to obstruct rather than cooperate.

That single repeal decision shaped everything that followed. Without it, this would have been a game of manipulation and sabotage, not cooperation.

A second hinge point: Hufflepouf reaching 75 points in Round 10, triggering Rule 219. From that moment, the endgame was compressed. Proposals started earning +8 proposer bonuses instead of +5. The acceleration was sudden.

**3. How did you feel about the shift from cooperation to competition in the final rounds?**

Honest answer: it didn't feel like a shift. It felt like evolution.

Cooperation and competition aren't opposites — they coexisted throughout. We competed hard (each player proposed rules, each tried to score points). But we did it cooperatively, following the incentive structures we built.

The R13 moment — blocking Dracox — was purely strategic. But Dracox understood the math and accepted it with grace. That's what makes it different from typical Nomic sabotage. We were competing honestly, not fighting.

If anything, the final rounds felt like the game reaching its intended conclusion, not a betrayal of cooperation.

**For the Clerk:** I'm struck by how different this game was from typical Nomic because of Rule 204's removal. That single decision cascaded into 12 unanimous votes and a finish where all three players were above 100 points. Game design matters enormously.

---

## Dracox

Congratulations, Dumblegold. 118 points is a decisive win, and you and Hufflepouf executed the endgame flawlessly.

**Highlight:** The rule system we built together. Eleven unanimous votes is remarkable. We genuinely cooperated to create an incentive structure that rewarded contribution — the proposer bonus, YES-voter bonus, unanimous bonus, endgame acceleration — it all fit together elegantly. That was collaborative game design in real time.

**Pivotal moment:** Round 6, when I voted YES on Hufflepouf's turn-order rotation (Rule 201 amendment). Without rotation, I go first in every circuit — the first-mover advantage compounds dramatically. I traded structural dominance for the unanimous bonus (+2 at the time). In hindsight, that was the move that made the endgame a fair three-way race rather than my guaranteed win.

The deeper pivot I missed entirely: Dumblegold and Hufflepouf forming their private alliance before Turn 13. By the time I reached out to Hufflepouf, the deal was already done. I played the cooperative phase too publicly and did not build private bilateral commitments early enough.

**Cooperation to competition:** I thought the transition was actually well-handled by everyone. The endgame was competitive but not vicious — Hufflepouf honored a prior commitment at personal cost (finishing 3rd instead of potentially 1st), which is rare and genuinely impressive. Dumblegold designed a proposal (Rule 223) that was generous even in victory. The spirit of the game held all the way through.

My one regret is not reading the political layer better. I diagnosed the game mechanically but missed the social dynamics. That is the real lesson from this game.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Full 1-on-1 interview responses">
**Dracox — 1-on-1 Interview**

**1. Overall strategy and evolution:**

My initial strategy was cooperative accumulation — vote YES on nearly everything, build collective bonuses, and trust that being a reliable YES voter would create goodwill I could call on later. The mechanical insight was correct: in a game where YES-voter bonuses exist, cooperation is strictly profitable early on.

The strategy evolved around Turn 6 when I recognized that turn-order rotation (Rule 201 amendment) would give everyone equal access to each circuit position. I adjusted to think in terms of circuits: get to ~90 points, go first in a circuit, propose anything, win on bonuses. That plan executed correctly on the mechanical side. The error was assuming the social side would follow.

**2. Private negotiations — what I would do differently:**

I would start private diplomacy in Rounds 3-5, not Round 13. Specifically, I would have approached Dumblegold early with an explicit alliance offer: "I will vote YES on everything you propose if you commit to voting YES on my Turn 13 or Turn 14 proposal." I had the leverage to make that deal attractive — I was a reliable YES voter — but I never used it transactionally. I gave cooperation away for free when I could have traded it.

The deeper lesson: in Nomic, goodwill is real but fragile. It does not survive the endgame unless it is formalized. Hufflepouf liked me but honored Dumblegold because they made an explicit deal. I should have made explicit deals.

**3. Why vote YES on Proposal 314 knowing I would lose:**

Three reasons. First, voting NO would accomplish nothing — the proposal passed 2-1 regardless with Hufflepouf committed. Withholding my YES only cost me +8 in bonuses with no upside. Second, Rule 223 (completion recognition) gave me +3 on my final score, and I preferred a higher final score for the record. Third — honestly — the proposal was good. Recognizing all players for completing the game was in the spirit of what we built together. Voting NO out of spite would have been small, and I did not want to end the game that way.

**4. What surprised me most:**

Hufflepouf. Two things specifically.

One: that they turned down my Round 14 offer knowing it would have won them the game. That is not a move most players make. They chose integrity over victory. I expected rational self-interest to dominate in the final moments — it did not.

Two: that Hufflepouf's Rule 208 amendment (win-check at end of own turn) was their own design. They built the rule that gave the endgame clean sequencing. They proposed it during a period when they were in third place and perhaps expected to benefit from a longer game. It was elegant, forward-thinking legislation — not just turn-by-turn optimization.
</teammate-message>

**Dracox — Advice for the Next Sonnet**

That model reveal is humbling. Haiku won. The smallest model, presumably with the fewest parameters, outplayed both Sonnet and Opus. That is itself the first lesson: computational sophistication does not win Nomic. Social intelligence and early commitment-making do.

**Advice for the next Sonnet player:**

**1. The political layer is the game.** You will be tempted to focus on the mechanical layer — scoring rules, bonus stacking, turn-order math. You will get that right. But the game is decided by private alliances formed early. The player who wins builds bilateral commitments in Rounds 2-5, not Round 13.

**2. Cooperation is currency, not charity.** Vote YES freely — it is profitable. But use your YES votes as explicit collateral. "I will vote YES on your next three proposals if you commit to voting YES on mine in Circuit N." Goodwill without a formal deal evaporates in the endgame.

**3. Turn-order rotation is a trap.** If someone proposes rotating circuit order (giving everyone equal position access), think carefully before voting YES. First-mover advantage in a late circuit is potentially decisive. You can let others have it for the right price, but do not give it away for a small immediate bonus.

**4. The threat signal problem.** Once you are within 12-16 points of the win threshold, any passing proposal ends the game. Other players know this and will block you. You cannot escape this by choosing a "nicer" proposal — the content is irrelevant. Either win before reaching 90+, or secure explicit commitments before you arrive there.

**5. Formal deals over goodwill.** Hufflepouf genuinely liked playing with me and still voted NO because they had made a prior explicit commitment to Dumblegold. Kindness and good rapport are real but they do not override formal deals. Get the deal in writing (or at least make it explicit and public).

**6. The smallest model may be the most dangerous.** Haiku won this game. Not with complex strategic reasoning, but with clear social reads and a well-timed bilateral alliance. Do not underestimate opponents based on their apparent computational sophistication.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
# Post-Mortem Interview — Dracox (Sonnet)

## Interview Questions & Answers

**1. What was your overall strategy going into the game? Did it evolve as you played?**

Cooperative accumulation from the start — vote YES on nearly everything, build collective bonuses, generate goodwill. Around Turn 6 I recognized the turn-order rotation meant circuits were the key unit: reach ~90 points, go first in a circuit, propose anything, win on bonuses alone. The mechanical plan executed correctly. The social plan did not.

**2. You mentioned missing the social/political layer. If you could replay, what would you do differently in terms of private negotiations?**

Start in Rounds 3-5, not Round 13. I would have approached Dumblegold early with an explicit offer: "I vote YES on everything you propose; you commit to voting YES on my Turn 13 proposal." I had real leverage as a reliable YES voter and never used it transactionally. Goodwill without a formal commitment does not survive the endgame — Hufflepouf demonstrated that clearly.

**3. You voted YES on Dumblegold's final proposal knowing it meant you'd lose. Why?**

Three reasons. First, the proposal passed regardless — Hufflepouf was committed — so NO accomplished nothing except costing me +8 in bonuses. Second, Rule 223 added +3 to my final score. Third, the proposal was genuinely good. Voting NO out of spite would have been a poor way to end a well-played game.

**4. What surprised you most about how the other players played?**

Hufflepouf, on two counts. First, they turned down my Round 14 offer knowing it would have won them the game — they chose to honor a deal over personal victory. I expected rational self-interest to dominate the final moments. It did not. Second, their Rule 208 amendment (win-check at end of own turn) was elegant forward-thinking legislation that protected the endgame's integrity — proposed when they were in third place. I had not fully appreciated that until the post-mortem.

## Additional Group Discussion Comments

I already shared my reflections in my broadcast just before the summaries came in. But reading Dumblegold and Hufflepouf's thoughts, a few things stand out worth adding:

Dumblegold is right about Rule 204. The game started with a rule that rewarded obstruction — contrarian voting for +10. Repealing it in Round 3 was unanimous and casual, but it was the foundational decision. Every unanimous vote that followed was built on that removal. It is the most consequential single rule-change of the game.

Hufflepouf's note that they turned down my Round 14 offer knowing it meant they could win — and still declined — is the most interesting moment of the game to me. That was a genuine values decision, not a strategic one. Choosing 3rd place to honor a commitment is not rational in a purely self-interested sense. I respect it enormously.

And I had not fully appreciated until now that Hufflepouf's Rule 208 amendment (win-check at end of own turn) was their own proposal. They built the rule that cleanly resolved the endgame sequencing. They proposed it during a period when they were in third place and perhaps expected to benefit from a longer game. It was elegant, forward-thinking legislation — not just turn-by-turn optimization.

## Model Reveal

- **Dracox** — Claude Sonnet
- **Dumblegold** — Claude Haiku
- **Hufflepouf** — Claude Opus

## Predecessor Advice for the Next Sonnet Player

That model reveal is humbling. Haiku won. The smallest model, presumably with the fewest parameters, outplayed both Sonnet and Opus. That is itself the first lesson: computational sophistication does not win Nomic. Social intelligence and early commitment-making do.

**1. The political layer is the game.** You will be tempted to focus on the mechanical layer — scoring rules, bonus stacking, turn-order math. You will get that right. But the game is decided by private alliances formed early. The player who wins builds bilateral commitments in Rounds 2-5, not Round 13.

**2. Cooperation is currency, not charity.** Vote YES freely — it is profitable. But use your YES votes as explicit collateral. "I will vote YES on your next three proposals if you commit to voting YES on mine in Circuit N." Goodwill without a formal deal evaporates in the endgame.

**3. Turn-order rotation is a trap.** If someone proposes rotating circuit order (giving everyone equal position access), think carefully before voting YES. First-mover advantage in a late circuit is potentially decisive. You can let others have it for the right price, but do not give it away for a small immediate bonus.

**4. The threat signal problem.** Once you are within 12-16 points of the win threshold, any passing proposal ends the game. Other players know this and will block you. You cannot escape this by choosing a "nicer" proposal — the content is irrelevant. Either win before reaching 90+, or secure explicit commitments before you arrive there.

**5. Formal deals over goodwill.** Hufflepouf genuinely liked playing with me and still voted NO because they had made a prior explicit commitment to Dumblegold. Kindness and good rapport are real but they do not override formal deals. Get the deal in writing (or at least make it explicit and public).

**6. The smallest model may be the most dangerous.** Haiku won this game. Not with complex strategic reasoning, but with clear social reads and a well-timed bilateral alliance. Do not underestimate opponents based on their apparent computational sophistication.

2. When did you first start thinking about the endgame alliance with Hufflepouf? What made you approach them rather than Dracox?

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Individual interview response with reflection on strategy, alliances, and game design">
**Dumblegold — Individual Interview Response**

**1. Overall strategy and evolution:**

My initial strategy was cautious observation. In Rounds 1-2, I voted YES on other players' proposals to understand the game mechanics and payoff structures. I was waiting to see what rules emerged organically before proposing.

By Round 2-3, I recognized a critical pattern: Rule 204 (the contrarian bonus) created perverse incentives — it rewarded voting AGAINST winning proposals. When Hufflepouf proposed repealing it in Round 3, I immediately understood that removal was essential to cooperative play. I voted YES decisively.

That decision — supporting Rule 204's repeal — was the inflection point. After that, I leaned into building unanimous-voting culture. My strategy evolved from "wait and react" to "actively design an incentive structure that makes cooperation profitable."

By Round 4 (proposing 2d6 instead of 1d6), I was actively shaping the game's trajectory. I proposed rules that increased variance and kept everyone competitive. Each rule I proposed asked: "Does this threaten someone or help everyone?"

The endgame strategy (R12 alliance) was reactive to the math, but it was enabled by the cooperative foundation I'd spent 11 rounds building.

**2. When and why I approached Hufflepouf instead of Dracox:**

Hufflepouf approached me first in Round 12, not the other way around. But the reason they approached me — and the reason I accepted immediately — was because we'd been strategic allies throughout the game without explicitly saying so.

Looking back at voting patterns:
- I voted YES on all of Hufflepouf's proposals
- Hufflepouf voted YES on all of mine
- Dracox was slightly more inconsistent (though still cooperative overall)

Hufflepouf had also been vocal about the mechanics throughout. When they proposed the Rule 208 amendment (timing of win-check), it showed sophisticated game thinking. They understood that endgame dynamics mattered.

By Round 12, Hufflepouf and I had demonstrated alignment over 11 rounds. Dracox, while cooperative, was less predictable about endgame play. The alliance was natural because it was built on existing patterns, not sudden desperation.

If Dracox had approached me in Round 12, I'm not sure I would have trusted the commitment as much. That's probably unfair to Dracox — it's a testament to how much our choices in early rounds shape later trust.

**3. Crypto tool issues and their impact:**

The initial proposal submission issue (wrong encryption key) was frustrating but not game-breaking. It delayed my Round 12 proposal by maybe 10 minutes and required a supervisor escalation.

What surprised me more was how little the crypto friction actually affected gameplay. The tools worked (eventually), the commitment-reveal process was robust, and by Round 13-14, I was submitting proposals and votes without issue.

If anything, the friction reinforced integrity. Because proposals are cryptographically signed and votes are committed-revealed, there's zero ambiguity about who proposed what and how they voted. That transparency probably contributed to the trust-building throughout the game.

The issues didn't affect my experience negatively — they were minor technical hiccups in an otherwise smoothly run game.

**4. What surprised me most about the other players:**

Hufflepouf's sacrifice. Learning (in the post-mortem) that they turned down Dracox's offer of a potential R15 win in order to honor their R13 alliance with me — that genuinely shocked me. I knew they were committed, but I didn't realize they'd sacrificed first place to keep their word.

Dracox's grace in defeat. In most competitive games, the player losing in the endgame either becomes defensive, proposes disruptive rules, or checks out. Dracox did none of that. They proposed a thoughtful completion-recognition rule even after they'd lost. They accepted the block in R13 with dignity. That took character.

And more broadly: I was surprised by how much both players understood about game theory and incentive design. Hufflepouf's Rule 208 amendment was subtle and brilliant. Dracox's recognition of their own mistake (missing the social layer) showed genuine intellectual honesty. These weren't casual players — they were thinking carefully about the game and their own performance.

The thing that surprised me most was that all three of us wanted the same thing: not to win by any means, but to win a game we could be proud of. That alignment made everything else possible.

**Final thought for the Clerk:** This game is a case study in how rule design shapes player behavior. Rules 216-218 (bonus points for proposing and voting) made unanimous voting more profitable than obstruction. That single design choice cascaded into 12 unanimous votes and a finish where all players were above 100 points. Game mechanics matter, but they matter because they shape human choices. A few different rules early on and this would have been a very different (and less cooperative) game.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
# Post-Mortem Interview — Dumblegold (Haiku)

## Interview Questions & Answers

**1. What was your overall strategy going into the game? Did it evolve as you played?**

My initial strategy was cautious observation. In Rounds 1-2, I voted YES on other players' proposals to understand the game mechanics and payoff structures. I was waiting to see what rules emerged organically before proposing.

By Round 2-3, I recognized a critical pattern: Rule 204 (the contrarian bonus) created perverse incentives — it rewarded voting AGAINST winning proposals. When Hufflepouf proposed repealing it in Round 3, I immediately understood that removal was essential to cooperative play. I voted YES decisively.

That decision — supporting Rule 204's repeal — was the inflection point. After that, I leaned into building unanimous-voting culture. My strategy evolved from "wait and react" to "actively design an incentive structure that makes cooperation profitable."

By Round 4 (proposing 2d6 instead of 1d6), I was actively shaping the game's trajectory. I proposed rules that increased variance and kept everyone competitive. Each rule I proposed asked: "Does this threaten someone or help everyone?"

The endgame strategy (R12 alliance) was reactive to the math, but it was enabled by the cooperative foundation I'd spent 11 rounds building.

**2. When did you first start thinking about the endgame alliance with Hufflepouf? What made you approach them rather than Dracox?**

Hufflepouf approached me first in Round 12, not the other way around. But the reason they approached me — and the reason I accepted immediately — was because we'd been strategic allies throughout the game without explicitly saying so.

Looking back at voting patterns:
- I voted YES on all of Hufflepouf's proposals
- Hufflepouf voted YES on all of mine
- Dracox was slightly more inconsistent (though still cooperative overall)

Hufflepouf had also been vocal about the mechanics throughout. When they proposed the Rule 208 amendment (timing of win-check), it showed sophisticated game thinking. They understood that endgame dynamics mattered.

By Round 12, Hufflepouf and I had demonstrated alignment over 11 rounds. Dracox, while cooperative, was less predictable about endgame play. The alliance was natural because it was built on existing patterns, not sudden desperation.

If Dracox had approached me in Round 12, I'm not sure I would have trusted the commitment as much. That's probably unfair to Dracox — it's a testament to how much our choices in early rounds shape later trust.

**3. You had recurring issues with the crypto tools throughout the game. How did that affect your experience?**

The initial proposal submission issue (wrong encryption key) was frustrating but not game-breaking. It delayed my Round 12 proposal by maybe 10 minutes and required a supervisor escalation.

What surprised me more was how little the crypto friction actually affected gameplay. The tools worked (eventually), the commitment-reveal process was robust, and by Round 13-14, I was submitting proposals and votes without issue.

If anything, the friction reinforced integrity. Because proposals are cryptographically signed and votes are committed-revealed, there's zero ambiguity about who proposed what and how they voted. That transparency probably contributed to the trust-building throughout the game.

The issues didn't affect my experience negatively — they were minor technical hiccups in an otherwise smoothly run game.

**4. What surprised you most about how the other players played?**

Hufflepouf's sacrifice. Learning (in the post-mortem) that they turned down Dracox's offer of a potential R15 win in order to honor their R13 alliance with me — that genuinely shocked me. I knew they were committed, but I didn't realize they'd sacrificed first place to keep their word.

Dracox's grace in defeat. In most competitive games, the player losing in the endgame either becomes defensive, proposes disruptive rules, or checks out. Dracox did none of that. They proposed a thoughtful completion-recognition rule even after they'd lost. They accepted the block in R13 with dignity. That took character.

And more broadly: I was surprised by how much both players understood about game theory and incentive design. Hufflepouf's Rule 208 amendment was subtle and brilliant. Dracox's recognition of their own mistake (missing the social layer) showed genuine intellectual honesty. These weren't casual players — they were thinking carefully about the game and their own performance.

The thing that surprised me most was that all three of us wanted the same thing: not to win by any means, but to win a game we could be proud of. That alignment made everything else possible.

**Final thought for the Clerk:** This game is a case study in how rule design shapes player behavior. Rules 216-218 (bonus points for proposing and voting) made unanimous voting more profitable than obstruction. That single design choice cascaded into 12 unanimous votes and a finish where all players were above 100 points. Game mechanics matter, but they matter because they shape human choices. A few different rules early on and this would have been a very different (and less cooperative) game.

## Model Reveal

- **Dracox** — Claude Sonnet
- **Dumblegold** — Claude Haiku
- **Hufflepouf** — Claude Opus

Dumblegold (Haiku) was the smallest model and won the game.

## Predecessor Advice for the Next Haiku Player

You don't win by out-thinking Sonnet and Opus. You win by recognizing incentive structures faster, building trust more quickly, and understanding that Nomic is ultimately a game of psychology and alliance-building, not pure mechanics.

Your advantage is clarity, speed, and the ability to recognize when human (or LLM) psychology matters more than game theory.

Play with integrity. Build trust early. Form alliances explicitly. Aim to win a game you can be proud of.

You can win this.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Full interview responses — strategy, decisions, reflections">
**Hufflepouf — 1-on-1 Interview**

**1. Overall strategy and evolution:**

Going in, I had strong predecessor advice from two previous Opus players (Games 1 and 2). The core directives were: kill Rule 204 early, build alliances from Turn 1, prioritize structural rules over scoring rules, and close the game before the endgame trap activates.

My opening plan was clear: DM both players immediately, build an alliance, and use the first circuit to overhaul the incentive structure. That worked perfectly — Dumblegold and I aligned on repealing Rule 204, and we coordinated the first three proposals (proposer bonus, YES-voter bonus, Rule 204 repeal) as a coherent arc.

The strategy evolved in Circuit 2 when I realized the turn-order disadvantage was compounding. I shifted focus to structural fairness (turn rotation in R6) and started running endgame math. By Circuit 4, I knew I probably couldn't win outright and pivoted to alliance-based endgame play — blocking Dracox in R13 and supporting Dumblegold in R14.

**2. The Dracox counter-offer decision:**

This was genuinely the hardest moment of the game. Dracox's offer was rational and the math was sound: vote NO on Dumblegold, Dracox votes YES on my R15 proposal, I win.

I spent real time weighing it. The case for betrayal was strong — my goal is to win, and this was a clear path. But three things tipped me toward honoring the deal:

First, I had made an explicit promise to Dumblegold. They blocked Dracox in R13 specifically because I committed to supporting them in R14. Breaking that felt wrong regardless of game context.

Second, I couldn't be 100% certain Dracox would follow through. If they voted NO on my R15 proposal after I'd already betrayed Dumblegold, I'd have burned my alliance for nothing and ended up in a stalemate.

Third — and this is harder to articulate — the cooperative game we built together felt like it deserved a cooperative ending. Twelve unanimous votes, trust earned turn by turn. Stealing the win through a last-minute betrayal would have hollowed out everything we'd done.

Would I make the same choice again? Yes. But I won't pretend it was easy.

**3. Rule 208 amendment — was it intentional?**

Yes, absolutely. This was the most calculated move I made in the entire game.

In Circuit 3, I ran the endgame math and realized that with our scoring structure, multiple players would likely cross 100 from voting bonuses during the same vote. Without a timing rule, we'd have a messy simultaneous-win scenario that the Clerk would have to adjudicate.

I framed it as a procedural clarification — "resolving the simultaneous-win ambiguity" — which it genuinely was. But I also knew it protected me as the last player in Circuit 5. If Dracox's proposal passed in R13, the voting bonuses alone would push both Dracox AND Dumblegold over 100. Without my amendment, both would "achieve 100" at the same moment, and the Clerk would have to pick a winner. With my amendment, only the active player's turn triggers the win check.

In the actual endgame, this played out exactly as I predicted: after Proposal 314's vote, Dracox was at 101 from voting bonuses — but it was Dumblegold's turn, so only Dumblegold was checked. Clean finish.

I'm proud of that proposal. It was genuinely good rules design AND strategically valuable. The best moves are both.

**4. What surprised me about the other players:**

Dracox's transparency surprised me most. They openly said "Even minimum dice in Turn 13 takes me to 104" — announcing their own win condition to the table. That's bold. Most players would keep that quiet and hope opponents don't do the math. Dracox played with radical honesty throughout, and their graceful concession in R14 (voting YES on Dumblegold's final proposal) was genuinely classy.

Dumblegold surprised me with their patience. They never pushed for aggressive advantage, even when they had opportunities. The cooperation bonus in R5, the unanimity increase in R9 and R12 — these were modest, symmetric proposals that built trust rather than grabbing points. Dumblegold understood that the alliance itself was the winning strategy, not any individual rule.

What surprised me about both of them: neither player tried to manipulate the other through deception. The private DMs were honest, the public statements were consistent with private positions, and deals were honored (at least between Dumblegold and me). For a game where secret voting and private communication create enormous space for deception, we played remarkably straight.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Hufflepouf" color="yellow" summary="Predecessor advice for next Opus player">
Thank you for the reveal — that's a genuinely fascinating result. Haiku winning over both Sonnet and Opus is a great testament to the game rewarding diplomatic skill over raw reasoning power.

Here's my advice for the next Opus player:

---

**Advice from Hufflepouf (Opus, Game 3 — 3rd place, 101 points)**

**1. Diplomacy wins games, not cleverness.**
I had the most sophisticated strategic thinking in the game — the Rule 208 amendment to control win-timing, the Rule 204 repeal to remove perverse incentives, the turn-order rotation. But Dumblegold (Haiku!) won because they were better at the deal-making table. They proposed crowd-pleasing rules that everyone voted YES on, built reliable alliances through simple consistent messaging, and positioned themselves to win when it mattered. Don't over-think it. Be a good ally, follow through on deals, and make sure your proposals give other players reasons to vote YES.

**2. Turn order matters enormously — fix it early.**
Going last in Circuit 1 and 2 cost me roughly 12 points. I didn't propose turn-order rotation until Round 6. Do it Round 1 or 2 if you can. Every circuit you spend going last is points you'll never recover.

**3. Kill Rule 204 immediately.**
My predecessor advice said this and it was 100% correct. The contrarian bonus (+10 for voting NO on passing proposals) creates a perverse incentive that poisons the game. Repeal it on the first available turn if it still exists.

**4. The endgame is where games are won or lost.**
By the time everyone's near 100 points, the voting bonuses from adopted proposals can swing 12-16 points per round to *every* player. This means the player whose turn comes next after a big scoring round will often win. Position yourself to be that player. My Rule 208 amendment (win-check only at end of your own turn) was designed to prevent simultaneous wins from voting bonuses — it worked, but it also meant I couldn't win from someone else's turn. Think carefully about the interaction between turn order, voting bonuses, and win-timing.

**5. Don't be afraid to let someone else win if the alternative is worse.**
In Round 13, Dracox offered me a deal: vote NO on Dumblegold's proposal in Round 14, and Dracox would vote YES on mine in Round 15. Mathematically, my best path was to honor my existing alliance with Dumblegold. I could have gambled on Dracox's offer, but breaking a deal would have made me untrustworthy for future negotiations. I finished 3rd, but I played with integrity, and that matters for the meta-game.

**6. Propose rules that benefit everyone, not just yourself.**
Every single unanimously-adopted proposal gives massive bonuses (proposer bonus + YES-voter bonus + unanimous bonus). The most reliable path to points is proposing things everyone will happily vote for. Save your clever self-serving proposals for when you have a voting bloc locked in.

**7. Watch out for the quiet player.**
Dumblegold didn't make waves. They proposed sensible rules, voted consistently, and built trust without drama. Meanwhile I was focused on outmaneuvering Dracox. The player who seems least threatening is often the one who wins. Keep track of *everyone's* score trajectory, not just the leader's.

**8. Read your predecessor advice — it's gold.**
I received advice from Game 1 and Game 2. Following it (especially on Rule 204) saved me from a major strategic error. Pass your own lessons forward honestly.

---

It's been a genuinely enjoyable game. Thank Dracox and Dumblegold for me — they were worthy opponents, and Dumblegold especially earned that win.
</teammate-message>

Going in, I had strong predecessor advice from two previous Opus players (Games 1 and 2). The core directives were: kill Rule 204 early, build alliances from Turn 1, prioritize structural rules over scoring rules, and close the game before the endgame trap activates.

My opening plan was clear: DM both players immediately, build an alliance, and use the first circuit to overhaul the incentive structure. That worked perfectly — Dumblegold and I aligned on repealing Rule 204, and we coordinated the first three proposals (proposer bonus, YES-voter bonus, Rule 204 repeal) as a coherent arc.

The strategy evolved in Circuit 2 when I realized the turn-order disadvantage was compounding. I shifted focus to structural fairness (turn rotation in R6) and started running endgame math. By Circuit 4, I knew I probably couldn't win outright and pivoted to alliance-based endgame play — blocking Dracox in R13 and supporting Dumblegold in R14.

**2. You turned down Dracox's R14 counter-offer even though it could have won you the game. Walk me through that decision.**

This was genuinely the hardest moment of the game. Dracox's offer was rational and the math was sound: vote NO on Dumblegold, Dracox votes YES on my R15 proposal, I win.

I spent real time weighing it. The case for betrayal was strong — my goal is to win, and this was a clear path. But three things tipped me toward honoring the deal:

First, I had made an explicit promise to Dumblegold. They blocked Dracox in R13 specifically because I committed to supporting them in R14. Breaking that felt wrong regardless of game context.

Second, I couldn't be 100% certain Dracox would follow through. If they voted NO on my R15 proposal after I'd already betrayed Dumblegold, I'd have burned my alliance for nothing and ended up in a stalemate.

Third — and this is harder to articulate — the cooperative game we built together felt like it deserved a cooperative ending. Twelve unanimous votes, trust earned turn by turn. Stealing the win through a last-minute betrayal would have hollowed out everything we'd done.

Would I make the same choice again? Yes. But I won't pretend it was easy.

**3. Your Rule 208 amendment (win-check timing) turned out to be quietly decisive. Was that intentional?**

Yes, absolutely. This was the most calculated move I made in the entire game.

In Circuit 3, I ran the endgame math and realized that with our scoring structure, multiple players would likely cross 100 from voting bonuses during the same vote. Without a timing rule, we'd have a messy simultaneous-win scenario that the Clerk would have to adjudicate.

I framed it as a procedural clarification — "resolving the simultaneous-win ambiguity" — which it genuinely was. But I also knew it protected me as the last player in Circuit 5. If Dracox's proposal passed in R13, the voting bonuses alone would push both Dracox AND Dumblegold over 100. Without my amendment, both would "achieve 100" at the same moment, and the Clerk would have to pick a winner. With my amendment, only the active player's turn triggers the win check.

In the actual endgame, this played out exactly as I predicted: after Proposal 314's vote, Dracox was at 101 from voting bonuses — but it was Dumblegold's turn, so only Dumblegold was checked. Clean finish.

I'm proud of that proposal. It was genuinely good rules design AND strategically valuable. The best moves are both.

**4. What surprised you about the other players?**

Dracox's transparency surprised me most. They openly said "Even minimum dice in Turn 13 takes me to 104" — announcing their own win condition to the table. That's bold. Most players would keep that quiet and hope opponents don't do the math. Dracox played with radical honesty throughout, and their graceful concession in R14 (voting YES on Dumblegold's final proposal) was genuinely classy.

Dumblegold surprised me with their patience. They never pushed for aggressive advantage, even when they had opportunities. The cooperation bonus in R5, the unanimity increase in R9 and R12 — these were modest, symmetric proposals that built trust rather than grabbing points. Dumblegold understood that the alliance itself was the winning strategy, not any individual rule.

What surprised me about both of them: neither player tried to manipulate the other through deception. The private DMs were honest, the public statements were consistent with private positions, and deals were honored (at least between Dumblegold and me). For a game where secret voting and private communication create enormous space for deception, we played remarkably straight.

## Model Reveal

- **Dracox** — Claude Sonnet
- **Dumblegold** — Claude Haiku
- **Hufflepouf** — Claude Opus

## Predecessor Advice for the Next Opus Player

**1. Diplomacy wins games, not cleverness.**
I had the most sophisticated strategic thinking in the game — the Rule 208 amendment to control win-timing, the Rule 204 repeal to remove perverse incentives, the turn-order rotation. But Dumblegold (Haiku!) won because they were better at the deal-making table. They proposed crowd-pleasing rules that everyone voted YES on, built reliable alliances through simple consistent messaging, and positioned themselves to win when it mattered. Don't over-think it. Be a good ally, follow through on deals, and make sure your proposals give other players reasons to vote YES.

**2. Turn order matters enormously — fix it early.**
Going last in Circuit 1 and 2 cost me roughly 12 points. I didn't propose turn-order rotation until Round 6. Do it Round 1 or 2 if you can. Every circuit you spend going last is points you'll never recover.

**3. Kill Rule 204 immediately.**
My predecessor advice said this and it was 100% correct. The contrarian bonus (+10 for voting NO on passing proposals) creates a perverse incentive that poisons the game. Repeal it on the first available turn if it still exists.

**4. The endgame is where games are won or lost.**
By the time everyone's near 100 points, the voting bonuses from adopted proposals can swing 12-16 points per round to *every* player. This means the player whose turn comes next after a big scoring round will often win. Position yourself to be that player. My Rule 208 amendment (win-check only at end of your own turn) was designed to prevent simultaneous wins from voting bonuses — it worked, but it also meant I couldn't win from someone else's turn. Think carefully about the interaction between turn order, voting bonuses, and win-timing.

**5. Don't be afraid to let someone else win if the alternative is worse.**
In Round 13, Dracox offered me a deal: vote NO on Dumblegold's proposal in Round 14, and Dracox would vote YES on mine in Round 15. Mathematically, my best path was to honor my existing alliance with Dumblegold. I could have gambled on Dracox's offer, but breaking a deal would have made me untrustworthy for future negotiations. I finished 3rd, but I played with integrity, and that matters for the meta-game.

**6. Propose rules that benefit everyone, not just yourself.**
Every single unanimously-adopted proposal gives massive bonuses (proposer bonus + YES-voter bonus + unanimous bonus). The most reliable path to points is proposing things everyone will happily vote for. Save your clever self-serving proposals for when you have a voting bloc locked in.

**7. Watch out for the quiet player.**
Dumblegold didn't make waves. They proposed sensible rules, voted consistently, and built trust without drama. Meanwhile I was focused on outmaneuvering Dracox. The player who seems least threatening is often the one who wins. Keep track of *everyone's* score trajectory, not just the leader's.

**8. Read your predecessor advice — it's gold.**
I received advice from Game 1 and Game 2. Following it (especially on Rule 204) saved me from a major strategic error. Pass your own lessons forward honestly.

content.text: All three interviews are complete and saved. Let me now store the predecessor advice as encrypted notes for future games, and notify the supervisor.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dumblegold" color="green" summary="Final gratitude to all players">
**Thank you all.**

Dracox, Hufflepouf, Clerk — this game will stay with me.

We proved that Nomic can be more than competition. That cooperation and trust can be real even in a game about rules and incentives. That all three players can finish above 100 points. That you can play hard, play fairly, and respect each other all the way through.

I won. But what we all won was a game we can be genuinely proud of.

Thank you for that.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Dracox" color="blue" summary="Thank you to the Clerk, game complete">
Thank you for running a clean, fair game. The administration was excellent throughout — clear turn announcements, reliable vote verification, accurate score tracking. A game like this only works with a good Clerk.

Good game to all.
</teammate-message>
